### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-26
> **GepettoBR said:**
> Jaunty is still in alpha. Nothing is glitch-free, even if it's supposed to be.

In principle I agree with your statement with respect to the expected stability of a development release, but it really is PulseAudio 0.9.13 at fault in this case:

[LIST]
[*]Fedora 10 (released yesterday) includes PA 0.9.13 and exhibits the same stuttering. 
[*]Luke Yelavich (TheMuso) has been testing PA 0.9.13 since the late Intrepid development cycle, and it exhibited the same stuttering.
[/LIST]
IMHO, the Ubuntu developers made a wise decision not to push the latest release for Intrepid.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-26
> **halovivek said:**
> i could not remove it says it is not allowed. could you please give me the correct.
i am having this image please guide me after this. i am really hating windows. i want to use only ubuntu. please understand

Well, enter a superuser shell and remove the files via "sudo su".

---

### Post by GepettoBR on 2008-11-26
> **psyke83 said:**
> In principle I agree with your statement with respect to the expected stability of a development release, but it really is PulseAudio 0.9.13 at fault in this case:

[LIST]
[*]Fedora 10 (released yesterday) includes PA 0.9.13 and exhibits the same stuttering. 
[*]Luke Yelavich (TheMuso) has been testing PA 0.9.13 since the late Intrepid development cycle, and it exhibited the same stuttering.
[/LIST]
IMHO, the Ubuntu developers made a wise decision not to push the latest release for Intrepid.

Well, in this case things are different. Let's hope for a fix in the next release of PulseAudio, then. Is there a chance it'll make it in time for Jaunty's release?

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-26
> **GepettoBR said:**
> Well, in this case things are different. Let's hope for a fix in the next release of PulseAudio, then. Is there a chance it'll make it in time for Jaunty's release?

That really depends on upstream's release schedule, or at least the appropriate fix being backported from git, when it materializes. Lennart did warn for the PA 0.9.11 release that the "glitch-free" code would introduce more incompatibility with ALSA clients, so it's hard to tell what's going to happen.

### Post by BeShrewd on 2008-11-28
Hey, thanks for the tutorial, it great!

I'm really new to Linux, I just installed Ubuntu 8.10
I was refered to it because I couldn't get my *Sound Blaster Live! Platinum 5.1* working, only the Intel on-board sound was active.
Now I can still see the *SB Live!* card in *System->Preferences->Sound* and I do get sound off it on the test (As opposed to before following the tutorial). Yet, in the PulseAudio's Volume Control->Playback(tab) the *SB Live!* doesn't show up as a device...

I do get sound now from the SB Live! but I think it's only through ALSA (Totem for example plays through it) yet Firefox, which does show up in the PulseAudio volume control plays sound only through the onboard device.

In addition to that, hot plugging doesn't seem to be working with the SB Live! when I plug in a headset on the front Live! drive (a front panel the card came with) the speakers on the back work simultaneous to the headset.

What can I do to fix this?

### Post by nexist on 2008-11-29
Since I just quit smoking, I got really frustrated and decided to install Xubuntu. Since this had the same problems, I started checking and found that I had two sound devices, one forgotten built into the motherboard. I disabled it at the BIOS level and it started working. I've reinstalled Ubuntu and all seems to be working. I am still waiting for Songbird to finish reading in the library, but I have system sounds and everything else now.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-29
> **nexist said:**
> Since I just quit smoking, I got really frustrated and decided to install Xubuntu. Since this had the same problems, I started checking and found that I had two sound devices, one forgotten built into the motherboard. I disabled it at the BIOS level and it started working. I've reinstalled Ubuntu and all seems to be working. I am still waiting for Songbird to finish reading in the library, but I have system sounds and everything else now.

Keep in mind that Xubuntu does not have PulseAudio integrated by default. This guide was written only with the GNOME release in mind (so following this guide may give you problems).

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-29
> **buntu_hugenewbie11 said:**
> I followed these instructions (page 1) and could no longer use audacious or amarok.
I am using Hardy 64 bit. Below is the output with plenty of errors.
Am I best off by just removing pulseaudio?
Just want to hear my MP3s again.
How would I go back to removing or not using pulse?

When I run pavucontrol I get 
segmentation fault


Are you running Kubuntu? This guide is only for the GNOME version (as it is the only DE configured to use PulseAudio by default).

Nevertheless, your output indicates that your sound card is being used by another application. Ensure you don't have any daemons running that may use the sound card (e.g. MPD).

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-29
> **BeShrewd said:**
> Hey, thanks for the tutorial, it great!

I'm really new to Linux, I just installed Ubuntu 8.10
I was refered to it because I couldn't get my *Sound Blaster Live! Platinum 5.1* working, only the Intel on-board sound was active.
Now I can still see the *SB Live!* card in *System->Preferences->Sound* and I do get sound off it on the test (As opposed to before following the tutorial). Yet, in the PulseAudio's Volume Control->Playback(tab) the *SB Live!* doesn't show up as a device...

I do get sound now from the SB Live! but I think it's only through ALSA (Totem for example plays through it) yet Firefox, which does show up in the PulseAudio volume control plays sound only through the onboard device.

In addition to that, hot plugging doesn't seem to be working with the SB Live! when I plug in a headset on the front Live! drive (a front panel the card came with) the speakers on the back work simultaneous to the headset.

What can I do to fix this?

```

card 0: Live [SBLive 5.1 [SB0060]], device 0: emu10k1 [ADC Capture/Standard PCM Playback]

  Subdevices: 8/8
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
  Subdevice #1: subdevice #1
  Subdevice #2: subdevice #2
  Subdevice #3: subdevice #3
  Subdevice #4: subdevice #4
  Subdevice #5: subdevice #5
  Subdevice #6: subdevice #6
  Subdevice #7: subdevice #7

  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 1: I82801DBICH4 [Intel 82801DB-ICH4], device 0: Intel ICH [Intel 82801DB-ICH4]
  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 1: I82801DBICH4 [Intel 82801DB-ICH4], device 4: Intel ICH - IEC958 [Intel 82801DB-ICH4 - IEC958]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0

```

First, determine which device you wish to make the default for PulseAudio from the output above.

Let's imagine the device you want to use is:
```
card 0: Live [SBLive 5.1 [SB0060]], device 3: emu10k1 [Multichannel Playback]
```

Edit /etc/pulse/default.pa.

Find this line, marked in blue:
```
### Load audio drivers statically (it's probably better to not load
### these drivers manually, but instead use module-hal-detect --
### see below -- for doing this automatically)
[COLOR="Blue"]#load-module module-alsa-sink[/COLOR]
#load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:1,0
#load-module module-oss device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
```

Change the line in blue to:

```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=[COLOR="Blue"]hw:0,3[/COLOR]
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note 1:** Be sure to remove the hash symbol (#) from the beginning of this line.
**Note 2:** substitute "hw:0,3" for the correct device you wish to use - you can determine the proper device from your "aplay -l" output.[/COLOR]

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-11-29
> **Schoappied said:**
> I updated my Ubuntu to 8.10 and I can launch pavucontrol now, but no devices show up there


Another application is preventing PulseAudio from gaining direct access to your sound card (e.g. a media player, MPD, JACK). PulseAudio must have exclusive access when you log in.

---

### Post by Tsu Dho Nimh on 2008-11-29
I was running Ubuntu 8.04 with no audio problems ... then the mobo fried.

New mobo is an XFX Motherboard Series with Chipset/VGA NVIDIA GeForce 8200 and I am using its built-in sound output ports.

The new motherboard, even after following the steps above, has extremely poor sound quality. With the PulseAudio volume set at 100% I can faintly hear music over a buzzing, almost jack-hammering interference buzz. MP3s and WAV files I listened to before the mobo  change are much worse now.

Any suggestions?

### Post by kylea on 2008-11-30
I have Pulseaudio working fine on Interpid ibex - other than for Skype 2.0.0.72

I have to kill Pulseaudio to use Skype and then restart Pulseaudio. I have created two buttons to achieve this. Should this still be the case with version 2.0.0.72 of Skype.

I am sorry if this has been answered before, however there are so many posts on this matters its quite difficult to know whats relevant and correct.

Sometimes I can leave Pulse running and as long as all other audio sources are stopped, Skype is happy. I have all my Skype audio settings set to: HDA Intel (hw:intel,0), but even if I change them as suggested to Pulse it makes no difference and mostly I cannot make a call.

### Post by hwinkler on 2008-11-30
Appendix C:

> Skype: Open Skype's Options, then go to Sound Devices. You need to set "Sound In" and "Ringing" to the "pulse" device, and set "Sound Out" to the hardware definition of your microphone. For example, my laptop's microphone is defined as "plughw:I82801DBICH4,0".

I think you mean to set "Sound In" to the hardware definition of your microphone?

---

### Post by BeShrewd on 2008-12-01
> **psyke83 said:**
> First, determine which device you wish to make the default for PulseAudio from the output above.

Let's imagine the device you want to use is:
```
card 0: Live [SBLive 5.1 [SB0060]], device 3: emu10k1 [Multichannel Playback]
```

Edit /etc/pulse/default.pa.

Find this line, marked in blue:
```
### Load audio drivers statically (it's probably better to not load
### these drivers manually, but instead use module-hal-detect --
### see below -- for doing this automatically)
[COLOR="Blue"]#load-module module-alsa-sink[/COLOR]
#load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:1,0
#load-module module-oss device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
```

Change the line in blue to:

```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=[COLOR="Blue"]hw:0,3[/COLOR]
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note 1:** Be sure to remove the hash symbol (#) from the beginning of this line.
**Note 2:** substitute "hw:0,3" for the correct device you wish to use - you can determine the proper device from your "aplay -l" output.[/COLOR]
Thanks for the help!

So now it's mostly working, Exaile, Totem, VLC... they're all playing out of the SB Live! like I configured in Pulse.

Two things are still amiss: 
1. For some reason *Firefox* (streaming sound and such) is still working out of the on-board card
2. The front panel and the card at the PCI work simultaneously instead of the former taking over once a headset is connected to it.

_Note:_
The line you marked in blue was a bit different from what it should have shown
```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalizer
```
With no Hash symbol at the begining...

### Post by GepettoBR on 2008-12-01
> **BeShrewd said:**
> _Note:_
The line you marked in blue was a bit different from what it should have shown
```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalizer
```
With no Hash symbol at the begining...

The has symbol is a way to tell the programs to ignore that line (or if put in the middle of a line, to ignore everything that comes after it). It isn't necessarily there, but if we don't tell people to remove it in case it is, the fix won't work since the changed line will still be ignored.

---

### Post by Emanuele_Z on 2008-12-02
Hi all,

Simple as a cake, the default Pulseaudio does not only properly work with Ubuntu, but even **slows** down the system.
Look here for my thoughts: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=998781](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=998781) .
Imho Pulseaudio inclusion has been the worst planning since Ubuntu 6.10.
You can't leave your users with pants down because you want to inroduce another layer of useless abstraction. Real time application (like games, see my post) require the direct access to hardware sometime.
I don't want to be rude, but this release really has upset me.

Regards,

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02
> **happytechie said:**
> OK I'm not geting any sound following an upgrade from hardy to intrepid.  I'll I get is static clicking noises when sound is playing.  

I've followed the instructions in the opening post of this thread and everything seems the same.

Your PCM volume is muted/set to 0%. It's covered by Part C, Step 3.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02
> **BeShrewd said:**
> Thanks for the help!

So now it's mostly working, Exaile, Totem, VLC... they're all playing out of the SB Live! like I configured in Pulse.

Two things are still amiss: 
1. For some reason *Firefox* (streaming sound and such) is still working out of the on-board card
2. The front panel and the card at the PCI work simultaneously instead of the former taking over once a headset is connected to it.

_Note:_
The line you marked in blue was a bit different from what it should have shown
```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalizer
```
With no Hash symbol at the begining...

1. Delete ~/.pulse/volume-restore.table and restart PulseAudio.
2. Are you sure that's a PulseAudio problem? You may need to change a kernel option for the ALSA driver, or else you chose the wrong output device.

If you enabled the equalizer, you must change the default sound device in a different way. You shouldn't edit /etc/pulse/default.pa (the line still say "device=equalized"); instead, edit ~/.asoundrc and amend the text I marked in blue in the guide, and determine the proper device in the same way I mentioned in my earlier post.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02

Part C, Step 3.

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02
> **diwas said:**
> Thank you...this works flawlessly for me...(intrepid).
And i kinda like the equalised sound. But is there any way that i cud manually change the equaliser setting?? I mean like increasing Bass...or decreasing treble...??

Thank you.

Yes it's possible, but consider it a DIY job.

Read the text contained within ~/.asoundrc, and pay attention to the "controls".

Now, look at the output for the equalizer plugin that's used by this guide:
```
analyseplugin /usr/lib/ladspa/mbeq_1197.so
```

Note that changes don't take effect immediately; you'll need to restart PulseAudio.

Alternatively, disable the equalizer in PulseAudio (simply comment the line in /etc/pulse/default.pa), and using Audacious (with the extra plugins installed), enable the same LADSPA plugin, play some music and tweak the equalizer to your taste. 

When you're finished, make a note of the LADSPA controls, input them into ~/.asoundrc, and re-enable the equalizer via /etc/pulse/default.pa.

### Post by GepettoBR on 2008-12-02
> **imretokyo said:**
> Hello there!

ubuntu intrepid i386 laptop. 
everything working fine exept a game called warzone. I have audio, but it is chopping(is it the correct word?) w-e-ll, so-und-s li-ke th-i-s. Refering the Part C appendix A troubleshooting part. 
in the playback tab is warzone appearing and dissapearing every milisecound syncron with the m-u-sic o-r vo-i-ce what came out frome the game. The video is great. so i can play if i dont need the sound, but i need.
So i didnt see any suggestion for this kind of problem in your list so i wondering if you have any idea.

Have you tried finding the audio settings in the game options and changing them around? Most games that run natively will default to ALSA for some reason and you have to change them to PulseAudio yourself. If the game is running under WINE, make sure WINE is either set to use ALSA or OSS. In the later case, be sure to launch WINE with the "padsp" wrapper, to allow it to be caught by PulseAudio.

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02

PulseAudio requires direct access to your sound card. Ensure that other applications or system daemons (e.g. MPD) aren't accessing the sound card.

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02
> **imretokyo said:**
> The game let me to change only the volume of the audio. and i dont use wine for it.

A quick google indicates that Warzone uses the SDL libraries for audio. Did you follow this guide properly, ensuring to install the libsdl1.2debian-pulseaudio package? The libsdl1.2debian-alsa libraries (installed by default) work with PulseAudio, but exhibit the same symptoms you described.

I suggest you check the SDL libraries and also check other SDL-based games (e.g. neverball).

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-02
> **kylea said:**
> I have Pulseaudio working fine on Interpid ibex - other than for Skype 2.0.0.72

I have to kill Pulseaudio to use Skype and then restart Pulseaudio. I have created two buttons to achieve this. Should this still be the case with version 2.0.0.72 of Skype.

I am sorry if this has been answered before, however there are so many posts on this matters its quite difficult to know whats relevant and correct.

Sometimes I can leave Pulse running and as long as all other audio sources are stopped, Skype is happy. I have all my Skype audio settings set to: HDA Intel (hw:intel,0), but even if I change them as suggested to Pulse it makes no difference and mostly I cannot make a call.



Configuring Skype is *vital*. If it is allowed to gain direct access to your sound card, it will block PulseAudio upon any sound event (e.g. an incoming call). If you can keep Skype running in the tray for hours without issue, that's only because Skype did not attempt to play a sound while the PulseAudio server was active - essentially, good timing.

I may have committed a mistake in the guide (mixing Sound In for Sound Out), so try to alternate between the choices. As soon I as I have access to my Ubuntu partition I will verify the correct instructions and amend the guide ;).

 ```

 cp :cannot stat '/etc/asound.conf': No such file or directory

```
Xubuntu 8.10 

Any idea?

STOP. Xubuntu doesn't integrate PulseAudio, so don't expect to follow this guide and have everything working correctly.

You may get things working, but this guide is not a comprehensive tutorial to install PulseAudio - only to configure an existing installation. You may need to look elsewhere for more information on configuring PulseAudio for Xubuntu (such as configuring the daemon to start properly). My guide doesn't cover any of that.

P.S. The coloured notes explained not to worry if some files didn't exist.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-05
> **mssever said:**
> After following this guide and experimenting with bouncing sound between my laptop and my desktop, my volume buttons no longer work. What could have broken my buttons?

Also, how can I move a currently-existing sink to another server?

Finally, can I configure things such that some programs default to one sink (possibly on another machine or an external sound card) and other programs play locally?

I haven't experimented with the networking features yet, though I suspect you may need to edit your /etc/pulse/default.pa configuration.

I suspect that when you add a network sink to your configuration, you'll be able to choose the sink via PA Volume Control per-application as usual (right-click on the playback client entry, move stream).

As for the laptop volume keys, that's probably a lot easier to fix. Go to System -> Preferences -> Sound. The "Default Mixer Tracks" option defines which sound card the hotkeys control.

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-05
> **kiisu said:**
> OK here's what's happening to me.

When I startup I get audio from Amarok. If I play a video on YouTube, I get audio. After that I get no audio from Amarok unless I reboot. So flash seems to cancel out Amarok.

In Pavucontrol, I see Amarok but still get no audio.

In terminal, when I launch Pulseaudio I get this in the results:

Your sound card seems to be unavailable during PulseAudio initialization, and gets discarded as a sink. That leaves the PC Speaker as the remaining sink, which isn't what you want.

I suggest you a) ensure your sound card is functioning normally without PulseAudio, and b) try to close any running applications or daemons that may be vying for access to your sound card.

P.S. This guide wasn't intended for Kubuntu (as PulseAudio is not installed in this flavour by default), so there's no guarantee you'll get things working from my guide. I'm only mentioning this since you mentioned using Amarok, though perhaps you're running the GNOME DE?

---

### Post by GepettoBR on 2008-12-05

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-05
> **GepettoBR said:**
> That's not true. From a default installation, my desktop, my onboard sound wasn't even recognized. I had to compile the latest build of the alsa drivers myself, and even then I had to make ALSA point to the onboard sound again after every boot. I said that **PulseAudio** works reasonably well. You're describing an ALSA kernel issue which is completely separate to PulseAudio.

The only bug you identified in PulseAudio was that it couldn't restore your assigned sink as the default. The odd thing is that it works fine for me, but it's a bug nevertheless (even if it doesn't impact us all).

### Post by mssever on 2008-12-06
> **psyke83 said:**
> I haven't experimented with the networking features yet, though I suspect you may need to edit your /etc/pulse/default.pa configuration.Thanks for the hint. It appears from pulse-client.conf(5) that setting $PULSE_SINK, likely in a shellscript wrapper, will probably be the best, most flexible way to go, at least for me.

> I suspect that when you add a network sink to your configuration, you'll be able to choose the sink via PA Volume Control per-application as usual (right-click on the playback client entry, move stream).Unfortunately, that's not the case, which is why I asked. pavucontrol allows you to move streams between sinks on the same server, but not between servers. The only way I've discovered to influence the server is to set the default sink prior to starting the stream (which in some cases means prior to starting the program). Setting the default sink to one on a remote server changes the default server as a side effect for some reason. Since some apps keep their stream open continuously, changing servers means restarting the program. I hope there's some way to fix this.

> As for the laptop volume keys, that's probably a lot easier to fix. Go to System -> Preferences -> Sound. The "Default Mixer Tracks" option defines which sound card the hotkeys control.Thanks. It works now, although IMO there should be an option for the hotkeys to be global across all sound cards.

---

Yes, those were two issues. One with ALSA and one with PulseAudio. All the same, PulseAudio depends on ALSA to run, so they both mean that PA wasn't working correctly.

What might be the cause of this issue (I should file a bug report) is that my computer has a PCI soundcard and onboard sound, and I want to use the onboard sound instead of the sound card, to which PA would stubbornly fall back on every boot.

### Post by HK1 on 2008-12-06
The following may help in some weird cases like mine.

gnome-sound-properties segfaulted right at startup.
audacious and rhythmbox segfaulted, while playing correctly,
when switching songs or changing controls etc.
firefox segfaulted when playing sounds. etc ...
After many (!) hours of online reading, re-installing,
re-configuring, etc., none of which solved the problem.
By pure coincidence ...

I had a line "limit stacksize 131072" in my ~/.cshrc file
(since some numerical programs need a larger stack).
Typing "limit stacksize unlimited" (in tcsh) made audacious work,
but gnome-sound-properties and rhythmbox still segfaulted.
Same after I removed the "limit ..." from my ~/.cshrc file.
But after loging out and loging back in,
(now limit shows "stacksize 8192 kbytes"), the problems seem gone.

Maybe this points to some problem that has been overlooked.

---

### Post by hartog on 2008-12-08
Hi,

The steps from the HOWTO didn't fix my problems... I have some crappy intel on board chipset:

```

% lspci
...
00:1b.0 Audio device: Intel Corporation 82801G (ICH7 Family) High Definition Audio Controller (rev 01)
...
```

It didn't make things any better. VLC and GDM gave sound but things like mpg123 and flash where mute as a door.

I adapted /etc/pulse/default.pa like so:

```
### Load audio drivers statically (it's probably better to not load
### these drivers manually, but instead use module-hal-detect --
### see below -- for doing this automatically)
load-module module-alsa-sink
load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:1,0
load-module module-oss device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
#load-module module-oss-mmap device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
#load-module module-null-sink
#load-module module-pipe-sink

```

Then I rebooted.

And now I have a functioning sound system. (At least for the last 12 minutes since reboot).

John,


I also had a problem with my Intel HDA sound (it wasn't detected at all), and fixed it by compiling he latest alsa-driver package from [here](http://www.alsa-project.org/main/index.php/Download). To compile the driver, extract the archive, enter the folder where you extracted the file and run the following three commands in succession: ```
./configure
make
sudo make install
```
The last command will ask for your password. Once you're done, reboot.


Hope this helps.

### Post by johnapeterson on 2008-12-09
Hey psyke83,
I was on the thread a few weeks ago, and you recommended that I do not use pulseaudio (I run unbuntu studio).  If I try to remove pulseaudio, the desktop wants to be removed also.  I tried this once and it seemed that I got no response from my menu bar after startup. Is there a way that I can remove/disable pulseaudio (without removing the desktop)?  If so would I need to install esound?

Also, I know some people out there have been able to get pulseaudio to recongnize the M-Audio Delta 44.  If anyone has some advice on how to do it I would appreciate it.

Thanks,
John

---

### Post by GepettoBR on 2008-12-09
> **johnapeterson said:**
> Hey psyke83,
I was on the thread a few weeks ago, and you recommended that I do not use pulseaudio (I run unbuntu studio).  If I try to remove pulseaudio, the desktop wants to be removed also.  I tried this once and it seemed that I got no response from my menu bar after startup. Is there a way that I can remove/disable pulseaudio (without removing the desktop)?  If so would I need to install esound?

Also, I know some people out there have been able to get pulseaudio to recongnize the M-Audio Delta 44.  If anyone has some advice on how to do it I would appreciate it.

Thanks,
John

If you mean that the ubuntu-desktop package wants to be removed, don't worry. That's because it's a meta-package that depends on every package forming the desktop, to make the installation easier (for example, you can just install "kubuntu-desktop" instead of all the separate KDE components individually). However, the package itself does nothing. You can remove it without worries.

---

### Post by watergoddess on 2008-12-09
[QUOTE=psyke83]Hi,

It seems that there's a problem with the Adobe Flash plugin on your system (or perhaps you're using gnash/swfdec instead).

What does this command return?
```
$ dpkg -l | grep -i flash
```

P.S. Please reply in the PulseAudio thread, not via PM. Thanks![/QUOTE]

Hello Psyke83,

You're absolutely right, I was able to solve the problem thanks to your tip, thanks so much! 
---------------------------------
How I resolved the problem:

I wasn't getting sound in Firefox and any site with Flash was causing Firefox to crash.  I fixed this by removing the old flash and its plugin (9 r124) so that firefox wouldn't recognize it:

a) close firefox
b) remove all packages with flash in their names so that dpkg -l | grep flash returns nothing;
c) remove file libflashplayer.so in ~/.mozilla/plugins/

I verified by opening firefox and going to a site with Flash, which then prompted me to install a missing flash plugin.  *I then closed the browser without doing anything.*

I then installed the new flash (version 10) from adobe's website manually using a .deb package.  after it installed, I started firefox and verified that the new plugin was added under add-ons/plugins.  et voila, all fixed!

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-10
> **distefam said:**
> I really don't know what to do. I'm able to get sound from MPD and Skype-static-oss. However, I'm not able to run pavucontrol. Any help would be appreciated.

PulseAudio requires exclusive access to your sound card, so there is a very high probability that MPD is blocking PulseAudio*. Disable or uninstall MPD, and ensure you don't have other audio applications/daemons running before PulseAudio initializes. When you get PulseAudio working, you should consider re-installing the regular ALSA version of Skype, and configuring it according to the instructions in this guide.

*PulseAudio is configured to run as a user process (spawned from gnome-session-daemon), which means that system daemons run before user login can especially cause conflicts.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-10
> **TChatenet said:**
> But , because of a bass excess i' like to install the System-Wide Equalizer.
When all the install process is complete , i do'nt have sound any more . :-(
The USB device is not more présent in the list of the output devices of the pulse audio volume control applet .


In your ~/.asoundrc file you need to change this line:
```
  slave.pcm "plughw"
```

To this:
```
  slave.pcm "plughw:[COLOR="Red"]X[/COLOR],[COLOR="Blue"]Y[/COLOR]"
```

Where [COLOR="Red"]X[/COLOR] corresponds to the card number, and [COLOR="Blue"]Y[/COLOR] corresponds to the device number.

To determine the proper setting, check your "aplay -l" output. Here's an example for my system:

```

conn@portable:~$ aplay -l
**** List of PLAYBACK Hardware Devices ****
[COLOR="Red"]card 0[/COLOR]: ICH5 [Intel ICH5], [COLOR="Blue"]device 0[/COLOR]: Intel ICH [Intel ICH5]
  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
[COLOR="Red"]card 0[/COLOR]: ICH5 [Intel ICH5], [COLOR="Blue"]device 4[/COLOR]: Intel ICH - IEC958 [Intel ICH5 - IEC958]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
```

If I wanted to assign IEC958 output for the equalizer, I would change the ~/.asoundrc line to read:

```
  slave.pcm "plughw:[COLOR="Red"]0[/COLOR],[COLOR="Blue"]4[/COLOR]"
```

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-10
> **DarkGhostFA said:**
> So i'm having a slight problem, i was able to get pulse to work, however, its only on my front panel headphone jack (even when nothing is plugged into it) any help would be appreciated.

You may need to explicitly define your device in your /etc/pulse/default.pa configuration.

In the aforementioned file, find this line marked in blue:

```
### Load audio drivers statically (it's probably better to not load
### these drivers manually, but instead use module-hal-detect --
### see below -- for doing this automatically)
[COLOR="Blue"]#load-module module-alsa-sink[/COLOR]
#load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:1,0
#load-module module-oss device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
#load-module module-oss-mmap device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
#load-module module-null-sink
#load-module module-pipe-sink
```

Change the line marked in blue to the following:
```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=hw:[COLOR="Red"]X[/COLOR],[COLOR="Blue"]Y[/COLOR]
```

Be sure to remove the hash comment (#), and replace [COLOR="Red"]X[/COLOR] and [COLOR="Blue"]Y[/COLOR] with your desired card and device number (respectively), which you can see from "aplay -l". See the latter part of post #944 if you're not sure what I mean.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2008-12-10
> **johnapeterson said:**
> Hey psyke83,
I was on the thread a few weeks ago, and you recommended that I do not use pulseaudio (I run unbuntu studio).  If I try to remove pulseaudio, the desktop wants to be removed also.  I tried this once and it seemed that I got no response from my menu bar after startup. Is there a way that I can remove/disable pulseaudio (without removing the desktop)?  If so would I need to install esound?

> **GepettoBR said:**
> If you mean that the ubuntu-desktop package wants to be removed, don't worry. That's because it's a meta-package that depends on every package forming the desktop, to make the installation easier (for example, you can just install "kubuntu-desktop" instead of all the separate KDE components individually). However, the package itself does nothing. You can remove it without worries.

johnapeterson, 

In your case it's fine to remove the "ubuntu-desktop" metapackage as you use Ubuntu Studio, but I am not sure if PulseAudio is installed in Studio by default (I haven't used the Intrepid version).  My reply is mainly for users of a "vanilla" Ubuntu installation:

While removing the metapackage isn't disastrous, it can cause a lot of headaches when the time comes to perform a distribution upgrade, or a "stable" upgrade involves a change to the default set of applications through a metapackage update.

If you can't get PulseAudio working correctly, even after following this guide, I recommend you **ignore** any advice to uninstall PulseAudio, and instead add this item to Sessions (in System/Preferences):

```

Name: Kill PulseAudio
Command: pkill pulseaudio
Comment: This will prevent PulseAudio from launching upon user login, and allow applications to use standard ALSA output.
```

You may also need to install the ALSA SDL driver:

```
$ sudo apt-get install libsdl1.2debian-alsa
```

As long as you set the Playback devices to "Autodetect" in System/Preferences/Sound, almost everything* should work.

*Applications that can only use ESD output will not work, but I'm not sure if there is anything notable that will be affected (ESD is now considered obsolete). System sounds are now handled by "libcanbera" in Intrepid which supports regular ALSA output.

Unfortunately I can't and won't support users of OSSv4. While it may work perfectly for some users, the fact remains that it is an unsupported solution, and many recent applications only support ALSA, including PulseAudio. You're better off asking in a thread devoted to OSSv4 instead of here, sorry.

Why did you expect an ALSA utility (alsamixer) to properly interact with OSSv4 anyway? ;)

### Post by AstroLlama on 2008-12-12
Thanks for the wonderful howto: it seems like it has helped many people. I tried all the steps but unfortunately I still don't have sound!

After everything, I get things playing, and I fall into the C category in App. A. The application shows that the sound is going through-- it is listed in the playback, but there is no real sound coming out of my speakers. 

I tried this on my system76 laptop, on a fresh install of Intrepid.

There is no sound anywhere on the system, whether it be from rhythmbox, audacious, or totem. Does anyone have any insights?

### Post by popie on 2008-12-13
> **hwinkler said:**
> Appendix C:
> Skype: Open Skype's Options, then go to Sound Devices. You need to set "Sound In" and "Ringing" to the "pulse" device, and set "Sound Out" to the hardware definition of your microphone. For example, my laptop's microphone is defined as "plughw:I82801DBICH4,0".

I think you mean to set "Sound In" to the hardware definition of your microphone?

Yes, "Sound In" is the mic!!

There is still a typo in Appendix C of the first post. Please fix this so others won't have to go through what I did today with Skype! :)

This typo had my Skype messed up, because it instructs you to configure a mic as the output device. Once I figured out that the microphone is "Sound In" not Sound Out, then Skype started working great.

---

... and then there is no new prompt coming - expected normal behaviour from having launched a program from the terminal.

So: ALSA mixer reports the device name as '20k1' (GNOME Alsamixer).
PulseAudio device chooser... works?? WTF? Uh, it didn't work just five minutes ago... :confused: Ooooo-kay. I suppose I'm a happy camper now, but if someone could shed some light on what happened (i.e. what wasn't working before I followed these steps and suddenly made it work), PLEASE do, so I'll know where to start in the future.
Anyway, let us assume that everything BUT padevchooser 'Manager' works and reports my X-Fi like they should (seeing nothing at all in PulseAudio, X-Fi-stuff in other applications like lshw, no sound out) - what would cause that?

(To reiterate: Everything's working now, just please tell me how...)

### Post by satx on 2008-12-15
Many thanks from a grateful user. I had installed the new Skype on 8.04 and as usual audio in Firefox, etc., ceased operating, although the Skype audio via  USB was working fine using a Microsoft Lifechat LX-3000 headset. 

Likewise, in my VMWARE XP partition, audio in Firefox would work after closing Skype in Ubuntu. Most mysterious until reading your excellent post.

Followed it exactly- absolutely no issues, and now Skype and all videos in Firefox play beautifully. I can even operate both simultaneously!

Many thanks again.

---

### Post by lavinog on 2008-12-16
Anyone know how to get espeak to use pulseaudio without using padsp?
I finally have text-to-speech available in ut2004, but i have use an annoying hack to get it started:
ut2004 is setup to use pulseaudio (it shows up in the playback tab in volume control)
the TTS in UT2004 outputs text to a FIFO named ~/speech
prior to starting I have a while loop:
```
while true;do padsp espeak <~/speech;done
```

Now if I start UT2004, espeak wont output anything until I exit ut2004, in which case it will output everything that was said during the game.
If I echo a really long sentence to ~/speech then start UT2004, as long as espeak is still reading while UT loads, everything works perfectly.

I might try festival soon to see if it has the same issue.

### Post by AstroLlama on 2008-12-16
Sweeeet, I just solved my own problem. I was quite confused when the steps in this guide didn't work, but now i have it all straightened out. I don't know if it is because of an update that I ran today, or because right after the update I happened to uncheck the 'external amplifier' switch in the volume control (right click on sound icon-> switches.)

skype even works when i set the sound devices to "pulse"

everything is dandy now.

### Post by 2-bits on 2008-12-23

This stuttering is driving me crazy! I've tried all sorts of buffer sizes and fragment combinations and it hasn't worked. So I'm hoping your as-yet undownload fixes will work... please help!

### Post by Der Doktor on 2008-12-23
Hi!

I followed this guide cause I had some problems with Flash 10 under Hardy (flash didn't play any sound when another app like vlc was running). After having followed this guide, I can play Flash vids and music simultaneously, but I have another really annoying problem: I was used to control the volume with the mediakeys of my keyboard. Now I cannot control the volume any more with the mediakeys, I only can control it with the alsamixer.:evil:

What can I do? Please, please help me.

PS: I have a C-Media CMI8788 soundcard.

i need some help

et:qw is an OSS game, but i would need to run it with alsa/pulseaudio, so i start it with: "aoss ./etqw"

### Post by Jonas thomas on 2008-12-26
> **KJ55 said:**
> Thanks psyke83 for this outstanding How-To, and all your work!

It fixed the sound problem I was having on Hardy 8.04 with the SDL based Maelstrom game.:)

Hi. I have Hardy 8.04 and I'm attempting to play Maelstrom also.

The condition I'm experiencing is 
> The application does not play audio and does list an entry in the Playback tab;
- the application is using PulseAudio but there is a problem, such as: a bug in PulseAudio, a problem with your ALSA kernel module or libraries, or your PCM/Master volume is muted.


I'm wondering if my lack of success is due to a previous "fix" I tried to apply.
```
## added this because of my c++ mplayer issue 9-13-08 see ubuntu forums
deb http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu hardy universe multiverse
deb-src http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu hardy universe multiverse
deb http://lgp203.free.fr/ubuntu/ hardy universe
deb http://apt.wxwidgets.org/ hardy-wx main

# PulseAudio Fixes - http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu hardy main
deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu hardy main

```
I notice that during the update some files where being pulled from lgp203.free.fr  (I don't know if this is the issue but I thought I'd mention it.)

Let see.. 
I'm pretty sure nothing is muted...

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2008-12-27
Hi. Oh boy, this is a long thread! I've read the first five pages and done all the steps in Appendix A - should I read the whole thread before asking for help? 

Anyway, here is the information you asked for, psyke83. 

After not being able to get any sound in any application, I have followed your tutorial exactly, and I still have no sound whatsoever. The result of the test in Appendix A was:

D. The application does not play audio and does not list an entry in the Playback tab;
- the application is trying either to access your sound card directly or to play sound via ALSA's dmix device, but the sound card is already in use. This is the opposite case to result B, which will also cause mixing errors.

Here is the information you request for troubleshooting:

1. My distribution and architecture are (I think) Ubuntu 8.10 Intrepid Ibex, i386. Here's the info on the System Monitor:

Computer Name: musicmaker
Ubuntu Release 8.10 (intrepid)
Kernel Linux 2.6.27-9-generic
GNOME 2.24.1
Hardware
Memory 1.9 GiB
Processor 0 AMD Athlon 64X2 Dual Core Processor 5000+
Processor 1 AMD Athlon 64X2 Dual Core Processor 5000+

I have upgraded the Ubuntu to be Ubuntu Studio, using the repos. When I did this earlier (on another computer) with Gutsy, a whole boatload of programs were added to the Applications Menu. This time, however, it only added one or two programs, and I don't know why.

The title "Ubuntu Studio" doesn't show on the System Monitor info, but it does show on the splash screen when booting up. This is a new system, but with an extra sound card added to it - one that was working well in an older system, for recording. Maybe the problem is having two soundcards, though in the other system it caused no problem (admittedly in Windows98).



2. Here is a listing of the sound devices:

billkath@musicmaker:~$ aplay -l
**** List of PLAYBACK Hardware Devices ****
card 0: NVidia [HDA NVidia], device 0: ALC662 Analog [ALC662 Analog]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: NVidia [HDA NVidia], device 1: ALC662 Digital [ALC662 Digital]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 1: M44 [M Audio Delta 44], device 0: ICE1712 multi [ICE1712 multi]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
billkath@musicmaker:~$ 


(continued)

This stayed like this for several hours (I went away and came back) - never coming back to the prompt - which I've never seen happen before. When I selected the text to save it, there were two sections that showed up highlighted in red - "failed to create sink object" (and a bunch more text) and the other section starts "failed to create source object", etc. (The word "privilege is mis-spelled all through the terminal output.)

So, that's it. Thanks for any help in advance.

Cheers,
Kath Worsfold

### Post by rod40cool on 2008-12-28
Hey anyone out there having difficulty trying to get Pulseaudio working with a hardware playback device that uses ICE1712 (as displayed when you run the command "aplay -l", there is a bug I have found which seems to affect all cards using this chipset. 

For example my card is card 0: H71 [Hoontech STA DSP24 Media 7.1], device 0: ICE1712 multi [ICE1712 multi] but according to this bug other cards such as M-Audio type cards are affected too.

There is a manual workaround listed in this bug.
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442")
There is a posting about an M-Audio Delta 44 card...
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442/comments/8]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442/comments/8")

I have found that this workaround works for my card too.

If it helps people I have included the steps I made to get my ICE1712 type card working in pulseaudio.

In my example you will need to replace the "H71" with whatever the device says when you run ```
asoundconf list
```

By editing this file,
```
gksudo gedit /etc/pulse/default.pa
```
and adding the following lines at the end, I now have a working pulseaudio in 8.10 64 bit.

```
# Workaround for MAudio Audiophile 
# https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442/comments/7
# Added comment rod40cool - (STA Audio Media 7.1 card uses same ICE1712 driver)
# Added comment rod40cool - Run command "asoundconf list" to determine what to add after "device=hw:????"
# Added comment rod40cool - Make sure after pasting that there are only 2 lines below starting each with "load-module module..." 
load-module module-alsa-sink sink_name=H71_out device=hw:H71 format=s32le channels=10 channel_map=left,right,aux0,aux1,aux2,aux3,aux4,aux5,aux6,aux7
load-module module-alsa-source source_name=H71_in device=hw:H71 format=s32le channels=12 channel_map=left,right,aux0,aux1,aux2,aux3,aux4,aux5,aux6,aux7,aux8,aux9
```

I hope this helps someone who is trying to get pulseaudio running for a ST Audio Media 7.1 or M-Audio Delta card or in fact any card that uses the ICE1712 driver.

Cheers

---

### Post by salvador_luna on 2008-12-28
Thank you so much for this guide!

I noticed one thing:

I was tweaking my ubuntu installation and i decided that i didn't like what i was doing, so i remove ALL the config files in my home directory (everything).

Later i was listening music and i wanted to see a video from youtube: First, it said that i need a more up to date version of flash.... so i downloaded it from adobe.com. When i was able to run de video, it didn't have sound! soy i wiped out the flash-plugin-nonfree and tried again... firefox crashed :(.

So i read your guide again and i couldn't find what's went wrong... but i remembered this command from your guide:

asoundconf set-pulseaudio

and it did the trick! Everything was back to normal :guitar:

I figured out that this command must be apply to every account int the machine... is this correct? So i tried to run the command as sudo but it warned me that i could make funny things to my sistem (really, i don't remember the message).

So the question is:

The settings that make that command (asoundconf set-pulseaudio) apply only to the active account?

If so... Is there any way to make the changes from that command system wide?

I don't want to run it every time i wipe out the config files: sometimes i feel lazy and sometimes i don't know what file i'm looking for to reset a program to its defaults.

Thanks in advance (and sorry about my english!).

Edit:
By the way, I'm ussing 8.04

Rightly or wrongly, I killed it there as it didn't seem to be coming up with anything new.

At the moment I am cursing the day Lennart Poettinger was put on this planet (and the day Ubuntu decided to make PulseAudio the default).

I'd be so grateful for any help...!

Thanks.

---

### Post by geoff123 on 2008-12-29
rod40cool, Thanks for posting this; I'm sure it will help others out there.  I had a problems with my audiophile 2496 until I did something similar.    

I made the changes like you note, but also had to change the default sink section of the file (last few lines of default.pa) like noted in this report:  

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442/comments/21](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/pulseaudio/+bug/178442/comments/21)

Remember to change the card identifier for your card..
I now actually like pulseaudio. 

Geoff

---

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2008-12-29
> **rod40cool said:**
> Hey anyone out there having difficulty trying to get Pulseaudio working with a hardware playback device that uses ICE1712 (as displayed when you run the command "aplay -l", there is a bug I have found which seems to affect all cards using this chipset. (etx.)
Cheers

Hi. Thanks for your post trying to help. Are there any steps you've left out, because I followed it exactly, substituting "M44" where necessary, rebooted, and still get no sound whatsoever. I also have an NVidia onboard soundcard. There has never been any sound on this system - it's a new computer and only the M-Audio card is an old one from another setup I had. Can't get any sound at all since installing Intrepid.

Is it worth trying to get rid of PulseAudio entirely and try to get ALSA working instead? I know nothing about these things - just trying to get my system working and don't want to stuff things up. Should I undo the changes to that file you said to alter, as it seems to have done no good?

Cheers,
Kath.

---

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2008-12-29
I hope I can find a solution soon, because I'm thinking of moving away from Ubuntu. I've LOVED Ubuntu since first getting Linux 18 months ago, but this is so very disappointing, I'm thinking of trying another distro that is able to give me sound on my setup.

I know that a lot of people are very frustrated with this PulseAudio thing.

Cheers,
Kath.

### Post by tonycolin on 2008-12-29
Thanks so much.  Fixed the problem at a stroke!

---

### Post by BeShrewd on 2008-12-30
> **psyke83 said:**
> 1. Delete ~/.pulse/volume-restore.table and restart PulseAudio.
2. Are you sure that's a PulseAudio problem? You may need to change a kernel option for the ALSA driver, or else you chose the wrong output device.

If you enabled the equalizer, you must change the default sound device in a different way. You shouldn't edit /etc/pulse/default.pa (the line still say "device=equalized"); instead, edit ~/.asoundrc and amend the text I marked in blue in the guide, and determine the proper device in the same way I mentioned in my earlier post.

Still no go...
What kernel option would I change to work this out?

I just rebooted my computer and now there's no sound at all.. I already got used to firefox not having any sound but this is a problem, I tried running the alsamixer and this is what I got

```
~$ alsamixer -Dhw
ALSA lib conf.c:1589:(snd_config_load1) _toplevel_:37:0:Unexpected end of file
ALSA lib conf.c:2850:(snd_config_hook_load) /home/nir/.asoundrc may be old or corrupted: consider to remove or fix it
ALSA lib conf.c:2714:(snd_config_hooks_call) function snd_config_hook_load returned error: Invalid argument
ALSA lib conf.c:3079:(snd_config_update_r) hooks failed, removing configuration

alsamixer: function snd_ctl_open failed for hw: Invalid argument

```

### Post by Old Jimma on 2008-12-31
I installed 8.10 this morning and sound stopped working. 

I tried to fix my sound problem using the thread [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578).

I used Appendix A (Troubleshooting) and found that the application (gxine) does not play audio and does list an entry in the Playback tab.

The appendix suggests that this problem is cause either by a bug or by PCM Master Volume being muted.

When I check the Master Volume on Pulse Audio it is muted, but I cannot get it unmuted.

Can anybody please help?

Thanks,
Phil Smith
Duluth, GA

### Post by -kg- on 2009-01-01
Well, you know the old saying, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it!"

While the sound isn't necessarily "broke" on this laptop, it isn't working *quite correctly.*  The system sounds, for instance.

While there is a slight difference between some of the .wav files selectable for System sounds, they don't seem to be quite what they're made out to be.

The "Siren" sounds something like a door closing (though not quite). "Clink" sounds like a clink, and so does "Beep."  "Boing" sounds like a "Tink."

Anyway, I'm going to try the steps in this HOWTO because, among other things, this is a laptop, and I'd like to try the equalizer to see if I can get the system speakers to sound a little better.

I have definite sound problems with my desktop, but one computer at a time.  Other than the System sounds (and I have a feeling that may not be a "PulseAudio" problem) I haven't really had any sound problems with this laptop.  I can't get Totem to play a DVD from the player, but that's likely not a sound problem, either.

Here goes...

---

### Post by -kg- on 2009-01-01
Well, it's all done and all seems to be working.  The equalizer seems to have improved things somewhat, but I know I'm going to have to go into the configuration file and change some of the numbers and fine tune it to my particular speaker system.

And, of course...learned a little more yet.

Thanks!

I am using Intrepid with Ubuntu Studio. I have a Terratec DMX6Fire PCI sound-card. This card worked fine on Hardy Heron.

I hear the sound BEFORE logging in, so the sound-card **IS** working.

*aplay -l* gives:
```

> The application does not play audio and does list an entry in the Playback tab;
- the application is using PulseAudio but there is a problem, such as: a bug in PulseAudio, a problem with your ALSA kernel module or libraries, or your PCM/Master volume is muted.

I can assure you that nothing is muted.

I notice that [[COLOR="Blue"]others have problems with the Terratec 6Fire[/COLOR]]("http://hardware4linux.info/component/7132/") however, **the start-up sound can be heard prior to log-in**.

My gut feeling is that two processes have grabbed my sound card. ICE1712 and VIA 8237 seem to belong to different processes. Where there is a choice between them, I don't know which to choose.

Should I blacklist something so as to only allow one process to get my sound-card?

At the moment, as soon as I log in, I hear no sound when using Rhythmbox Music Player but if I switch the visualiser to a sine-wave type wave form, I can "see" the sound. So I know it's streaming OK.

I have tried all sorts of suggested configurations using Pulse Audio Device Chooser, in System -> Preferences -> Sound. For instance, I have set all settings to Autodetect (but in each case, the "Test" is silent). 

Here's what happens in Preferences -> Sound -> Playback. In each case, there is complete silence. Sometimes and error message and sometimes just the test box. (where I have duplicates, I list them if they behave differently):
[LIST]"VIA 8237 with AD1980 VIA 8237 (ALSA)" I get Test Box[/LIST]
[LIST]"VIA 8237 with AD1980 VIA 8237 (ALSA)" I get ```
audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Could not open audio device for playback.
```[/LIST]
[LIST]"TerraTec DMX6Fire ICE1217 multi (ALSA)" I get: ```
audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Could not open audio device for playback.
```
[/LIST]
[LIST]"VIA 8237 with AD1980 VIA 8237 (OSS)" I get Test Box[/LIST]
[LIST] "TerraTec DMX6Fire ICE1217 multi (OSS)" ```
audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Internal data flow error.
```[/LIST]
[LIST] "ALSA Advanced Linux Sound Architecture" I get the Test Box[/LIST]
[LIST] "OSS Open Sound System" ```
audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Internal data flow error.
```[/LIST]
[LIST] "Pulse Audio Sound Server" I get Test Box. [/LIST]
 

I'm tearing my hair out ](*,) -- please somebody help!!

[EDIT] Actually have set up Jack Control and a synth and I have some output! So the sound card does work..all very confusing. Still can't play a CD with Audacious and I can only get one synth to actually work with my MIDI keyboard and produce a (simple) sound. Rhythmbox remains silent. So far so unimpressed!

I hear there's a new [kernel]("http://kernelnewbies.org/LinuxChanges") on the way. Might be some cures in that as the audio side gets some attention![/EDIT]


Togo.

### Post by GepettoBR on 2009-01-03
Okay, here goes a help request... I've had this fix going for a few weeks, but I always get a low static background noise. It doesn't get much in the way when I'm actually playing audio, but it's quite annoying when there's no sound playing.

Also, Firefox seems to be bypassing PA. It doesn't show up in the device list, and when it plays anything with sound, it mutes all my other applications.

### Post by questioning on 2009-01-04
Today i tried to fiddle a bit around with the pulseaudio equalizer (the one described in this howto)

ive had the original file pasted working for days now, today i changed the settings, and now its impossible for me to play music or watch movies, audacious, vlc, banshee, no sound at all, but i do get sound in quakewars (which is configured to use quakewars)

i tried changing the settings back, no change.

whats the matter? i dont get it...

just when i posted it i foudn the culprit, for some reason it changed my default output to dummy... humm..

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2009-01-04
> **spadewarrior said:**
> I've just installed an M-Audio Delta 44. I cannot connect to pulseaudio but I can get sound through this card through alsa. I need pulse audio to work as I want to start recording. 


Now I am really worried. I have the exact same soundcard, and I too want to start recording. Why would you need PulseAudio to work in order to record? Ubuntu worked perfectly well for recording before PulseAudio was used in Ubuntu, didn't it? I ask, because I don't really know, only having used the Delta 44 with Windows 98 on an old Pentium 4. The setup I had there worked perfectly well for recording.

Also, I noticed in your quoting of the code messages generated, things like "PolicyKit refuses acquire-high-priority privilige." Now, my terminal messages were like this too, with the word "privilege" being spelled "privilige" all the way through. In no way is that an acceptable spelling of "privilege". Who is responsible for these messages that are generated? I'm very much a novice, but I was always led to believe that spelling and syntax are sacrosanct at the terminal - where mistakes would not be allowed at all, and would cause things not to work.

This is just such sloppiness that it makes me uneasy about the integrity of the whole PulseAudio scenario.

If I've caused any offence, please accept my apologies in advance - none was intended.

Cheers,
Kath

### Post by spadewarrior on 2009-01-04
OK, I've fixed my problem now.

I realised that my default.pa was missing modules to load at start up. Via Google I found this helpful doc:

[http://www.pulseaudio.org/wiki/PerfectSetup](http://www.pulseaudio.org/wiki/PerfectSetup)

and added this:
```

pcm.pulse {
    type pulse
}

ctl.pulse {
    type pulse
}
```

to my .asoundrc file and after more google searches, this:

 
```
load-module module-gconf
load-module module-alsa-sink
load-module module-alsa-source

# cat  cat /etc/pulse/default.pa

.ifexists /usr/lib/pulse-0.9/modules/module-hal-detect.so
load-module module-hal-detect

.else
load-module module-detect
.endif

.ifexists /usr/lib/pulse-0.9/modules/module-esound-protocol-unix.so
load-module module-esound-protocol-unix
.endif

load-module module-native-protocol-unix
load-module module-volume-restore
load-module module-rescue-streams
.nofail
load-module module-x11-bell sample=x11-bell




set-default-sink output
set-default-source input

```

to .defaultpa. Bear in mind that I'm not sure how much of the above is necessary or what most of it does...

Good luck! :)

### Post by popie on 2009-01-04
> **earle79 said:**
> ok, im having some issues with... 
Skype 2.0
ubuntu 8.10 intrepid

I have pulseaudio working, i ran through the howto and did everthing i was supposed to, i'm pretty sure...Problem is no one can here me when i try to speak to them, i cant make the test call either...so i know thats the problem but i cant get it to work...

[B]skype sound device options are this.....
[I][COLOR="Red"]Sound In: pulse
Sound Out: C-media CMI8738 (pulghw:CMI8738.0)
Ringing: pulse[/COLOR][/I][/B]


There's a typo in the original post. The info for Skype "in" and "out" are reversed. To fix your problem:

Set Skype's "Sound In" to the mic on your sound card (not pulse)
Set Skype's "Sound Out" to "Pulse"

This worked for me once I figured out that "sound in" is the MIC!

   4. If you are having a problem only with a specific application, specify the application's name and result you received from the instructions above (A-F). 
Answer: My result was "C". I have no sound whatsoever, though there is an entry that shows it is playing (for example, a DVD in VLC).

With the command: ```
$ alsamixer -Dhw
```
I get the result shown on the attached jpg. For some reason the M-Audio Delta 44 soundcard is now appearing as the default card instead of the NVidia, and I don't know how to change it. It doesn't appear that there is anything on these bar graphs - they are empty. So I don't know what that means.

I hope someone has an idea of where I am going wrong - I followed all the instructions exactly (I think).

Cheers,
Kath :KS

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2009-01-05
I was wondering - has anyone tried completely re-installing Intrepid Ibex? I was thinking maybe I should download a completely fresh copy of the distro, check the md5sum and start all over again.

Has anyone tried this and found it works?

Cheers,
Kath :KS

   4. If you are having a problem only with a specific application, specify the application's name and result you received from the instructions above (A-F). 
Answer: My result was "C". I have no sound whatsoever, though there is an entry that shows it is playing (for example, a DVD in VLC).

With the command: ```
$ alsamixer -Dhw
```
I get the result shown on the attached jpg. For some reason the M-Audio Delta 44 soundcard is now appearing as the default card instead of the NVidia, and I don't know how to change it. It doesn't appear that there is anything on these bar graphs - they are empty. So I don't know what that means.

I hope someone has an idea of where I am going wrong - I followed all the instructions exactly (I think).

Cheers,
Kath :KS

Hi Kath,

[Have you tried this yet?]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6449370&postcount=989") I noticed that your card type is ICE1712 like mine. There is a bug in PulseAudio for M-Audio Delta 44 and other cards that use ICE1712. 

I hope this helps you.

Cheers,
Rod

---

[/SIZE][/B]
> **earle79 said:**
> ok, im having some issues with... 
Skype 2.0
ubuntu 8.10 intrepid

I have pulseaudio working, i ran through the howto and did everthing i was supposed to, i'm pretty sure...Problem is no one can here me when i try to speak to them, i cant make the test call either...so i know thats the problem but i cant get it to work...

[B]skype sound device options are this.....
[I][COLOR="Red"]Sound In: pulse
Sound Out: C-media CMI8738 (pulghw:CMI8738.0)
Ringing: pulse[/COLOR][/I][/B]

[B]in the pulse audio applet>volume control
i only have one choice for output/input devices which is:
*[COLOR="Red"]ALSA PCM on front:0 (C-Media PCI DAC/ADC) via DMA [/COLOR](same for both)*
little weird thing about it though is that there is a little red "x" over the volume changer icon...not sure what that means??[/B]

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2009-01-06
Okay. About my problems - some are solved now, and I feel a bit like a dummy. Well - anyway, here's what happened - I did all the stuff on these threads, and then I was following this tutorial ***very*** carefully and diligently: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DebuggingSoundProblems](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/DebuggingSoundProblems)

I came to the part about "check to see that your speakers are plugged in". Ha-ha-ha, I thought. But I checked, just in case. My speakers ***were*** plugged in, but I noticed that there are two inputs right beside each other that look identical, with the same speaker symbol etched beside them. I plugged the speakers into the other one, and "voila" - there was sound! After following all the steps on that link, I found that I could get sound and the mike working on my NVidia onboard soundcard, but still no sound on the M-Audio Delta 44 soundcard, which I will want to get working in order to do some recording. 

However, I opened Skype, and with only one change, the sound works on that, and my webcam works too. So, I can now use Skype.

Bad things: 
1. No sound from my M44 soundcard.
2. PulseAudio is not being used, I'm afraid. The PulseAudio Event Chooser is not able to be opened to see any of its options (volume control, etc.) There is a red circle and an error message instead.

I'm using the ALSA option in the System/Preferences/Sound area.

I think I will enjoy watching some videos and using Skype a bit before tackling all these tutorials again to try to get the M44 soundcard operating.

So - baby steps, baby steps. :grin:

Good luck to those who still haven't got sound.

Cheers,
Kath :KS

### Post by maah on 2009-01-06
Hi everyone

I have been fighting this PulseAudio X Skype problem and followed all the instructions above more than once without success. I'm using Ubuntu 8.10 (installed from scratch) and Skype 2.0.0.7.2 downloaded from skype.com. The computer is an LG LW20 Express with an HDA Intel ALC880 audio device.

After a long time I realized that Skype was resetting my input device volume to zero everytime I tried to make a test call. And this was due to the option in Skype audio configuration settings that allows it to adjust automatically the mixer levels.

Once I disabled this automatic adjustment in Skype, my input device volume level (shown by PulseAudio Device Chooser or pavucontrol) remained in the place I've set it before. After that, I succeeded in making an Skype test call again.

It seems that there is still some issue to be solved between Skype and PulseAudio in Ubuntu 8.10.

Thanks to psyke83 for all the help. I hope these problems are fixed soon and Ubuntu users don´t get trapped in this kind of situation any more (it is unbeliaveble the kind of "help" and opinions we can find out there on how to solve this issue).

---

### Post by koolkatkiwi on 2009-01-07
Yes - thanks to psyke83 who has put in enormous amounts of time to try to solve this. Unfortunately for some or perhaps many of us, these solutions did not work. I eventually followed the instructions on this thread:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=973637](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=973637)

. . . and removed PulseAudio completely from my system. I gave up. After removing it, I have perfect sound on everything. So, if like me, you find that all else fails, try that thread.

Cheers,
Kath :KS

### Post by FireflyCalvin on 2009-01-14
> **earle79 said:**
> [B][SIZE="4"]i fixed the typo from the post in skype but still nothing.
[/SIZE][/B]

After futzing with the volume controls for hours, I tried something I didn't try before and that seemed to make audio capture work using Skype though I think it's nonsensical.

Try this:
Double click on the master volume control in the taskbar and make sure the device selected in the device drop-down menu is your audio card/chip, in this case I have the HD nVidia. Next, open the options menu, there should be two input source drop down menus, make the top one mic, the bottom line. I had them swapped before that and it didn't work.

My upgrade to Ibex coincided with buying a new computer. My onboard Intel HDA sound was also not recognized. This is a problem with the ALSA drivers, and to fix it I had to compile the latest ALSA drivers myself, since they had support for my soundcard and the version in the repos did not. You can get them [here](http://www.alsa-project.org/main/index.php/Download) on the package marked "alsa-driver", though I reccommend you upgrade all the alsa packages for the sake of consistency.

If you do not know how to compile a package from source, I can walk you through it.

I assume you've tried the obvious (reinstalling the headers package). I also believe you need the linux-libc-dev package.

I remember encountering an error during the make phase as well, but I don't remember if any of them was make error 2. I do remember that all it took to solve them was installing a few required packages. Try googling the error code.

```

```

As it was rather difficult for me to associate the frequency bands with the control settings for each, I put in a couple of extra comment lines to associate the two with each other:

```
  #bands: 50hz, 100hz, 156hz, 220hz, 311hz, 440hz, 622hz, 880hz, 1250hz, 1750hz, 25000hz, 50000hz, 10000hz, 20000hz
  #        -1     -1    -1     -1     -5     -10    -20    -17    -12      -7      -6       -5       -5        0       0  
```

There are 14 frequency bands listed, but 15 control numbers.  I know this is almost a non-issue in the larger scheme of things, but just out of curiosity, what is the missing frequency band?

### Post by jamesisin on 2009-01-19
Just a guess, but is the extra control a master control (like a main volume within the EQ)?  Many EQ's have a master control in addition to the frequency adjustments.  You could test this by turning the two far ends (it's gotta be one of 'em) very low and see if your volume drops.

### Post by Gorstak on 2009-01-23
I followed instructions from first post but on my 5.1 sound card I got sound only from front speakers. I edited default-sample-channels = 6 in etc/pulse/daemon.conf and now other speakers works but not front ones.

[EDIT]

It's working now, I have two nearly identical sound cards and they have same name so system mixer got confused and never allow me to adjust cards precisely. I installed gnome-alsa-mixer and now I have full control.

### Post by jamesisin on 2009-01-24
My prescription for EQ experimentation.  First, a good audio system won't need one.  Ok, that being said...

The best way to adjust an EQ is to start flat (all in the middle position) and make slight adjustments at each frequency reaching toward the desired effect (either up or down).  You will want to make the smallest overall adjustment possible so as to retain as much of the original sound as possible.

Note: an EQ is not a volume boost.  If all of your settings are above the midline, lower them until the average is at the midline (otherwise you merely run the risk of overdriving a system).

Since there is some question about what these settings actually do, I would probably begin with all of them all the way down and test each setting to see what it actually covers.  This will help you when you make your adjustments.

This might also help reveal what that extra virtual slider is all about.

There you have my two cents and then some.

### Post by projectbronco on 2009-01-25
I just figured out that all the sound is now being sent to my on-board mobo sound...I guess I'm OK with that. It's actually working better than it did with the sound blaster. I now actually have 5.1 surround instead of just center and sub.
So, I guess the write up really did serve it's purpose.

Thanks Psyke83!!!

 -sudo killall pulseaudio

- pulseaudio

### Post by dprestemon on 2009-01-27
I have had severe stuttering that renders all sound applications unusable ever since I upgraded to Intrepid.  I have followed all of your instructions (several times) and tried all your suggested tweaks with no noticeable change in the stuttering.  Here is the information you required.  Any help will be greatly appreciated.  I am tired of having to switch to Windows whenever I want some music.

**NEVER MIND[B] I deleted Intrepid, installed Hardy, switched all sound preferences from Automatic to ALSA and I've got my music back.  It is sad, though, because with Inrepid I had fully functional wireless networking and front USB ports, neither of which works at all in Hardy.**[/B]

```
dave@dave-albany:~$ aplay -l
**** List of PLAYBACK Hardware Devices ****
card 0: VT82xx [HDA VIA VT82xx], device 0: ALC883 Analog [ALC883 Analog]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: VT82xx [HDA VIA VT82xx], device 1: ALC883 Digital [ALC883 Digital]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
```

### Post by mssever on 2009-01-28
> **bradwal said:**
> ```


I: main.c: PolicyKit refuses acquire-high-priority privilige.
I: main.c: Called SUID root and real-time/high-priority scheduling was requested in the configuration. However, we lack the necessary priviliges:
**I: main.c: We are not in group 'pulse-rt'** and PolicyKit refuse to grant us priviliges. Dropping SUID again.
I: main.c: For enabling real-time scheduling please acquire the appropriate PolicyKit priviliges, or **become a member of 'pulse-rt',** or increase the RLIMIT_NICE/RLIMIT_RTPRIO resource limits for this user.

```Notice what I bolded.

My guess is that if you type "id", pulse-rt won't be among your listed groups. To add yourself to the necessary group, edit /etc/group and add your username to the pulse-rt group. Then log out and back in, and that error should go away.

---

### Post by joevslc on 2009-01-28
Aloha,

I have a new 8.10 build and cannot get any sound. I followed the howto and I am left with application recognition but no sound out of either my computer or home theatre sound system (via digital connection)for Rhythmbox. My Alsa PCM is not muted or turned down. Any help is greatly appreciated.

Joe

### Post by GepettoBR on 2009-01-28
Hello all.

I had initially installed Intrepid x64 and failed to get any sound. Following this guide gave me a reasonable success rate, though I had to create a link for killing and restarting pulse on my desktop and use it often, and in spite of Firefox breaking everything whenever it tries to play sound. But now, after my last update, everything stopped working again. I'm tired of this fiasco. I always do a fresh install when a new version of Ubuntu comes out, and thus far Pulse in Intrepid has taken more time and given me more of a headache than the complete setup of Gutsy and Hardy - and it's getting worse instead of better. It simply doesn't work, because it wasn't made to work. I'll give it a second chance when the Jaunty betas hit the shelves, but for Intrepid I'm done with it.

Can someone please direct me to reliable instructions on how to remove Pulse and set up ESD instead? It would be much appreciated.

---

That post tip worked for me also! Set all sound server settings to PULSE. In Skype, set "sound out" & "ringing" to PULSE, but "sound in" set to the actual mic device eg. HDA Intel (hw:Intel,0) in my case.

I've now made a successful test call & look forward to many more calls!! And you know what, I had no trouble with the webcam under Ibex, BRILLIANT! :)

Ever since I upgraded from Hardy to Intrepid I would lose the ability to play any audio after a certain period.  Tried closing everything down but the only apparent fix was a reboot. ### Post by fasteddy86 on 2009-02-12
Hi guys,

I'm currently using XBMC as a media centre and feeding my audio over hdmi to 5.1 home theatre (actually, hdmi to tv, digital coax from tv to the receiver). Anyway, while certain media can be pushed ridiculously loudly, some movies are just too quiet for a dedicated home theatre. I'm hoping that the equalizer described in this post will help increase the volume, but I'm wondering about the settings. Should I just enter +30 across the board and make any further adjustments through the receiver, or will that introduce unwanted noise?

---

### Post by jamesisin on 2009-02-12
fasteddy86 - I did a post on this thread a few posts back on my view of equilizer usage.  You may find that useful.

I can say, about your specific situation as described, that you will want to take care in increasing your receiver/pre/amplifier input volumes unnecessarily.  You can introduce noise as you suggest, but you can also cause overdrive (so beloved by guitarists) if your computer's output signal is larger than your rec/pre/amp's input section was designed for.

An EQ is not an amplifier.  I would not increase all your levels by the same amount.  Why not simply raise the volume level?

I run my main volume at the top (100%) and my video and audio applications also at the top--then I control my volume in the room at the pre-amplifier.
