### Post by El Viejo Lobo on 2009-02-13
> **psyke83 said:**
> [SIZE="4"]**HOWTO: PulseAudio Fixes & System-Wide Equalizer Support**[/SIZE]
*Overview:*

**06/01/2009 - Please note that I am currently abroad and will most likely not have time to answer posts for the next few months. This guide is still up-to-date.**

[COLOR="Red"]**Note 1. Intrepid users:** due to a bug in the ALSA libraries, your PCM mixer may occasionally become muted or reset to 0% volume. If you cannot hear sound - or hear a faint crackling - refer to Part C, Step 3.
**Note 2: OSSv4 users:** PulseAudio does not support OSSv4, so this guide will serve no purpose to you. If you have chosen to install OSSv4 and experience issues, you should seek guidance within the threads dedicated to OSSv4. I do not recommend users to install OSSv4 due to compatibility and support issues.
[/COLOR]
PulseAudio is an advanced sound server which has been included in **U**buntu (i.e. the standard GNOME version) since the release of Hardy Heron (8.04). Unfortunately, Hardy shipped with a suboptimal configuration of PulseAudio which has resulted in users experiencing various problems, ranging from sporadic crashes in Firefox to sound mixing being completely broken. PulseAudio in Intrepid should work by default, but it is quite possible that your configuration is suboptimal. For more information, refer to the **FAQ** below.

When you are ready to follow this guide, this is all you need to know:
Hardy users: Follow **Part A** & **B**.
Intrepid users: Follow **Part A** & **C**.
Additionally: 
[LIST]
[*]**Appendix A** gives general troubleshooting tips - if you have problems, start here.
[*]**Appendix B** gives useful information on the more advanced/technical features of PulseAudio.
[*]**Appendix C** gives information on how to properly configure specific applications that may not work with PulseAudio by default, including (but not limited to): WINE, Skype, and all OSS applications.
[*]**Appendix D** will show you how to enable equalized audio for *all* applications on your system - this is especially useful for laptop users who experience poor audio quality with their internal speakers.[/LIST]
[SIZE="4"]**Frequently Asked Questions**[/SIZE]
*The most common queries are answered here.*

**Q. What exactly is PulseAudio?**
A. From the [homepage]("http://www.pulseaudio.org/"):

Simplified: PulseAudio is responsible for playback and mixing of audio on your system. It is not a sound driver - in fact, it runs on top of the [Advanced Linux Sound Architecture]("http://www.alsa-project.org/") (ALSA). Aside from all the cool effects PulseAudio provides, it serves as a replacement for ALSA's virtual sound mixing device ([DmixPlugin]("http://alsa.opensrc.org/DmixPlugin"), or "dmix") - thus allowing multiple applications to share access to your sound card.

**Q. PulseAudio? Bleh! I don't want it on my system.**
A. Well... tough! PulseAudio is *already* installed and active on Hardy and Intrepid by default; it replaces ESD (ESound Daemon) for system sounds, and most of Ubuntu's default applications already use it (Totem, Rhythmbox, and any other applications using the GStreamer framework). Although some high-profile applications support PulseAudio natively (such as VLC and mplayer), most applications use plain ALSA or OSS output, and thus don't have native PulseAudio support.

**Q. If PulseAudio is already installed, why do I need this guide?**
A. While PulseAudio has been installed by default since Hardy Heron (8.04), we dropped the ball when it came to the configuration part. A quote from the main PulseAudio developer, [Lennart Pöttering]("http://0pointer.de/blog/projects/jeffrey-stedfast.html"):
When PulseAudio is running, it requires exclusive access to your sound card in order to work correctly as it assumes responsibility for mixing application's sounds instead of ALSA's "dmix" device. If you launch a "regular" application that does not have explicit PulseAudio support, it will most likely try to open the "Dmix" device - and this will deprive PulseAudio of control over the sound card. From the user's perspective, they will observe that audio mixing between applications is broken. 

PulseAudio includes ALSA plugins (within the package "libasound2-plugins") which are designed make regular ALSA applications remap audio to the PulseAudio server (and thus avoid mixing problems as described above). Unfortunately, Hardy Heron shipped without these plugins enabled (or even installed) by default, which is causing many, many audio mixing issues for users. To compound the problem, the version of these PulseAudio ALSA plugins in the Hardy repositories do not function correctly, so updated versions are required for ALSA applications to work correctly with PulseAudio.

By following this guide, your system will be configured to use these PulseAudio ALSA plugins for Hardy users (and updated versions of necessary packages will get installed from my [PPA]("https://launchpad.net/~psyke83/+archive")). Although Intrepid has these plugins installed and configured by default, following this guide is still worthwhile because a) it will ensure you have a clean PulseAudio configuration, and b) you will hopefully gain a better understanding of how PulseAudio works.

**Q. I'm glad to hear these issue are fixed in Intrepid, but why the hell aren't they fixed in Hardy already?**
A. The simplest answer to this question is: complexity. Hardy is a LTS (Long Term Support) release, and there is a very strict policy towards updates (SRU; even the most trivial of bugfixes are entered into a code review). In order to fix Hardy, many components will require updates and changes, including but not limited to: libflashsupport, ia32-libs, pulseaudio, libasound2, libasound2-plugins, flashplugin-nonfree, nspluginwrapper...

Up until the last moment of Hardy's development cycle, the PulseAudio ALSA plugins weren't functioning correctly, and Flash 9 absolutely would not work without the "evil" libflashsupport library (I say evil, because it caused frequent random crashes in Firefox) - and so it wasn't possible to enact the required changes before the final release. It's possible now, but there would require a huge amount of effort to review and apply these changes.

**Q. I followed your guide and PulseAudio still doesn't work!**
A. Refer to Appendix A and provide the requested information in your post.

**Q. I can't get Skype/WINE/an OSS application/XYZ working correctly with PulseAudio, what can I do?**
A. Some applications require some extra configuration, and some applications don't work with PulseAudio - please refer to Appendix C for information on specific applications.

**Q. Where can I find the appropriate bug reports related to these issues?**
A. If you click on a step number it will link to the appropriate bug report, if one exists.

**[SIZE="4"]Part A: Common instructions (Hardy & Intrepid)[/SIZE]**
*All users must must follow the steps in this section to guarantee a fully working PulseAudio configuration.*

1. Backup (and then delete) your previous configuration files: ```
$ mkdir ~/pulse-backup && cp -r ~/.pulse ~/.asound* /etc/asound.conf /etc/pulse -t ~/pulse-backup/
$ sudo rm -r ~/.pulse ~/.asound* /etc/asound.conf
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** Don't worry if some of these files did not exist on your system.[/COLOR]

2. Ensure you have Adobe Flash & the necessary PulseAudio libraries and configuration utilities installed:
```
 $ sudo apt-get install libasound2-plugins padevchooser libao-pulse libsdl1.2debian-pulseaudio flashplugin-nonfree
```

[3]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/192888"). Ensure the evil "libflashsupport" library is **not** installed:```
$ sudo apt-get remove --purge libflashsupport flashplugin-nonfree-extrasound
```

4. Open System/Preferences/Sound. In the Devices section, ensure that all "Sound playback" options are set to Autodetect. Set the "Sound capture" item to "PulseAudio". Close the application when you're finished.

5. Open the PulseAudio Volume Control application ("pavucontrol", or you can launch "Applications/Sound & Video/PulseAudio Device Chooser" and select Volume Control from this applet's menu). In the Output Devices section you will see a listing of the playback devices available on your system. Right-click on the entry that you desire to be made the default playback device on your system and enable the "Default" checkmark. Similarly, navigate to Input Devices, then right-click on the device you wish to set as your default input device (microphone), and ensure the "Default" setting is checked. Close the application when you're finished.

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** If you are greeted with the error "Connection failed: Connection refused", manually launch PulseAudio before opening the PulseAudio Volume Control application:
```
$ pulseaudio & pavucontrol
```[/COLOR]6. Continue to **Part B** if you are running Hardy Heron (8.04), or **Part C** if you are running Intrepid Ibex (8.10).

[SIZE="4"]**Part B: Hardy Heron (8.04)**[/SIZE]
[I]Follow the steps in this section only if you are running the Hardy Heron release.
Disclaimer: My PPA contains the necessary packages backported from Intrepid for: PulseAudio, ALSA, Flash 10 and nspluginwrapper. If you upgrade to a newer distribution, or Hardy receives official updates for any of these packages, you will **not** experience any issues.[/I]

[0]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/246911"). [COLOR="Red"]**64-bit users only**[/COLOR]: Install "getlibs" and some extra 32-bit libraries that are required for Flash 10 & Skype to function properly:```
$ wget http://www.boundlesssupremacy.com/Cappy/getlibs/getlibs-all.deb && sudo dpkg -i getlibs-all.deb && rm getlibs-all.deb
$ sudo getlibs -p libnss3-1d libnspr4-0d libcurl3 libasound2-plugins
```

1. Edit /etc/apt/sources.list:```
$ gksudo gedit /etc/apt/sources.list
```

If they don't already exist, add the following lines to the end of this file and save:```
# PulseAudio Fixes - http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu hardy main
deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu hardy main
```

2. Update your repositories and upgrade packages:```
$ sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** You may be warned that some packages cannot be authenticated - that's normal. Simply press "**y**" to confirm installation of unsigned packages.[/COLOR]

[3]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/198453"). Enable the PulseAudio ALSA plugins:```
$ asoundconf set-pulseaudio
```
[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** Please wait until you have upgraded packages in the previous step before running this command. My packages have a patch for "asoundconf" to ensure it enables the PulseAudio plugins correctly.[/COLOR]

4. Log out & back in for changes to take effect!

[SIZE="4"]**Part C: Intrepid Ibex (8.10)**[/SIZE]
[I]Follow the steps in this section only if you are running the Intrepid Ibex release.
Disclaimer: Currently there are no updated packages for Intrepid in my PPA (except for an updated nspluginwrapper package for i386 users). I have decided to keep this step in the guide in case I upload any important updates that will not make it into the official repositories. I will never upload any "risky" packages (i.e. highly untested backports), only upgrades that seem compelling and relatively stable.[/I]

1. Edit /etc/apt/sources.list:```
$ gksudo gedit /etc/apt/sources.list
```

If they don't already exist, add the following lines to the end of this file and save:```
# PulseAudio Fixes - http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=789578
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu intrepid main
deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/psyke83/ubuntu intrepid main
```

2. Update your repositories and upgrade packages:```
$ sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** You may be warned that some packages cannot be authenticated - that's normal. Simply press "**y**" to confirm installation of unsigned packages.[/COLOR]

3. Ensure that your sound card's PCM mixer is not muted or set to 0% volume (this appears to be a bug in Intrepid):

```
$ alsamixer [COLOR="Blue"]-Dhw[/COLOR]
```
[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** When the PulseAudio ALSA plugins are active, you must explicitly specify your hardware device in alsamixer (marked in blue above), otherwise it will open the PulseAudio mixer.[/COLOR]

4. Log out & back in for changes to take effect!

[SIZE="4"]**Appendix A - General Troubleshooting**[/SIZE]
*This section will outline some general troubleshooting steps you can perform to help identify your problem, and the information I need to help with your issues:*

[LIST=1]
[*]Close all applications that may be accessing the sound card.
[*]Open the "PulseAudio Device Chooser" from Applications/Sound & Video. Click on the applet icon, and click "Volume Control...". Click on the "Playback" tab.
[*]Launch the application you wish to test and allow it to play sound.
[*]Check PulseAudio Volume Control's Playback tab and see if the application displays an entry **while the application is (or should be) playing audio.**[/LIST]
Here are the possible results within the PulseAudio Volume Control's Playback tab:
[LIST=A]
[*]The application **does** plays audio and **does** list an entry in the Playback tab;
[COLOR="Blue"]- the application is using PulseAudio correctly.[/COLOR]
[*]The application **does** plays audio and **does not** list an entry in the Playback tab;
[COLOR="Blue"]- the application is either accessing your sound card directly or playing sound via ALSA's dmix device. This will prevent PulseAudio from working correctly & cause audio mixing errors.[/COLOR]
[*]The application **does not** play audio and **does** list an entry in the Playback tab;
[COLOR="Blue"]- the application is using PulseAudio but there is a problem, such as: a bug in PulseAudio, a problem with your ALSA kernel module or libraries, or your PCM/Master volume is muted.[/COLOR]
[*]The application **does not** play audio and **does not** list an entry in the Playback tab;
[COLOR="Blue"]- the application is trying either to access your sound card directly or to play sound via ALSA's dmix device, but the sound card is already in use. This is the opposite case to result B, which will also cause mixing errors.[/COLOR]
[*]The PulseAudio Volume Control application displays the error: "Connection failed: Connection refused";
[COLOR="Blue"]- the PulseAudio daemon is not running.[/COLOR]
[*]Other (please specify).
[/LIST]
[COLOR="Blue"]**Note: **Unless the application you are testing is known to be incompatible with PulseAudio, you should always expect result "A" on a properly configured system.[/COLOR]
 
If you require assistance with a particular application - or simply cannot get PulseAudio to work - provide the following information:

[LIST=1]
[*]Your distribution version and architecture (e.g. Hardy Heron i386, Intrepid Ibex amd64, etc.).


[*]A listing of your sound devices:```
$ aplay -l
```
[*]The verbose output from pulseaudio on your system:```
 $ pkill pulseaudio; sleep 2; pulseaudio -vv
```
[*]If you are having a problem only with a specific application, specify the application's name and result you received from the instructions above (A-F).
[/LIST]
[SIZE="4"]**Appendix B: Advanced PulseAudio Configuration**[/SIZE]
*This appendix will explain some of the more advanced/technical features of PulseAudio.*

**Q. Where are the PulseAudio configuration utilities?**
A. Providing you've followed this guide, you can gain access to all the utilities by launching "Applications/Sound & Video/PulseAudio Device Chooser". The applet's icon will appear in your notificiation tray - left-click to see the options.

The main applications you will want to check are the Manager (to see the current server status) and Volume Control (to manipulate the volume and stream settings).

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** Please don't mess with any of the options until you have established a working configuration by following this guide - otherwise it'll be a nightmare to isolate your problem.[/COLOR]

**Q. How can I tell if an application is using PulseAudio successfully?**
A. The application will give you result "A" from the troubleshooting steps of Appendix A.

**Q. How can I change the default playback and/or recording device for my system?**
A. Refer to Part A, Step 5.

**Q. Is it possible to change to a different playback and/or recording device for an individual application?**
A. Yes. Launch the desired application and play some sound (or begin recording), and open the PulseAudio Volume Control. Click the Playback or Recording tab (depending on which you want to change) and right-click on the desired application's entry. Choose the option "Move Stream..." and select the desired output device.

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** The volume level, sink (playback) and source (recording) settings will be saved automatically for every application you run, so PulseAudio should remember your settings. If you wish to view or delete these saved settings, they're stored in the file "~/.pulse/volume-restore.table".[/COLOR]

**Q. If I plug in my USB/Bluetooth headset, my external speakers no longer work!**
A. This is normal behaviour, as PulseAudio supports "hotplugging" of audio devices. If you plug in a new device, PulseAudio may choose it as the default sink (audio device). Also see Part A, Step 5. 

**Q. PulseAudio is working correctly, but I am noticing some stuttering on my system. Is there anything I can do to help?**
A. Edit the file /etc/pulse/daemon.conf:
```
$ gksudo gedit /etc/pulse/daemon.conf
```

Find the following lines (usually at the bottom):
```
default-fragments = [COLOR="Blue"]8[/COLOR]
default-fragment-size-msec = [COLOR="Blue"]10[/COLOR]
```

Try experimenting with different values for both of these entries. I can't tell you what values are optimal for your system, as each sound card has different buffer sizes and characteristics - therefore you'll need to use trial & error. The default fragment amount and size used by an untweaked PulseAudio installation is 4 and 25, respectively.
[COLOR="Blue"]
**Note 1:** you must restart pulseaudio for any configuration changes to take effect.
**Note 2:** If your system was stuttering in versions of Ubuntu prior to Hardy, then you could be suffering from an ALSA kernel issue - these instructions probably won't help.[/COLOR]

**Q. I'm unhappy about the audio quality / CPU usage of PulseAudio. How do I change this?**
A. For the record, PulseAudio should offer superior quality audio playback than ALSA's by default, as PulseAudio uses a higher quality audio resampler. This also means that PulseAudio may use more CPU usage compared to ALSA, unfortunately. If you wish to change the resampler:

Edit /etc/pulse/daemon.conf:```
$ gksudo gedit /etc/pulse/daemon.conf
```
Find the following line:```
resample-method = [COLOR="Blue"]speex-float-1[/COLOR]
```
You can change the resampler to any of the following, listed in descending order, from highest quality to lowest quality (and therefore, CPU usage): 

[SIZE="4"]**Appendix C: Application Compatibility Guide**[/SIZE]
*This appendix will explain how to configure specific applications that may require manual configuration to work with PulseAudio.*

**OSS applications**: You need to launch the application using the "padsp" wrapper. For more information, see "man padsp".
**Skype**: Open Skype's Options, then go to Sound Devices. You need to set "Sound Out" and "Ringing" to the "pulse" device, and set "Sound In" to the hardware definition of your microphone. For example, my laptop's microphone is defined as "plughw:I82801DBICH4,0".
**WINE**: Open the Wine Configuration application ("winecfg"). On the Audio tab, choose the ALSA driver, and leave everything else to default. If your sound stutters, choose the OSS driver instead, and use the "padsp" wrapper to launch the wine executable (via the terminal, or edit your shortcuts).
**Audacity (updated 11/12)**: From the [PerfectSetup]("http://www.pulseaudio.org/wiki/PerfectSetup") page:

**Everything else**: refer to the Third Party Applications section of the upstream [PerfectSetup]("http://www.pulseaudio.org/wiki/PerfectSetup#ThirdPartyApplications") wiki page. 

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** Many of the instructions on the PerfectSetup page are either outdated, too distro-agnostic to work on Ubuntu, or have been obsoleted by the fixes in this guide. You should ignore the advice for: ALSA applications, Flash, ESOUND, GNOME, GStreamer Applications, SDL, and Skype.[/COLOR]

[SIZE="4"]**Appendix D: System-Wide Equalizer**[/SIZE]
*In this section, we will configure PulseAudio to use equalized output, which is especially useful for laptops speakers which have very poor frequency response. If your sound is "tinny" or distorted at high ranges, then this will most likely enhance the quality of your audio.*
[COLOR="Red"]
**Warning 1:** Do **not** attempt to set up the equalizer until you have followed the other parts of this guide and verified that PulseAudio works correctly using "unmodified" output.
**Warning 2:** The equalizer may not work on 64 bit systems, as extra 32-bit libraries could be required. If it doesn't work, simply revert the changes you made.[/COLOR]

1. Install the required LADSPA plugins and tools:
```
$ sudo apt-get install swh-plugins ladspa-sdk
```

2. Edit ~/.asoundrc:
```
$ gedit ~/.asoundrc
```

Append the following text to the bottom of this file and save:

```
pcm.equalized {
  type plug
  slave.pcm "equalizer";
}

pcm.equalizer {
  type ladspa

  # The output from the EQ can either go direct to a hardware device
  # (if you have a hardware mixer, e.g. SBLive/Audigy) or it can go
  # to the software mixer shown here.
  slave.pcm "[COLOR="Blue"]plughw[/COLOR]"
  #slave.pcm "plug:dmix"

  # Sometimes you may need to specify the path to the plugins,
  # especially if you've just installed them.  Once you've logged
  # out/restarted this shouldn't be necessary, but if you get errors
  # about being unable to find plugins, try uncommenting this.
  path "/usr/lib/ladspa"

  plugins [
    {
      label mbeq
      id 1197
      input {
       #this setting is here by example, edit to your own taste
       #bands: 50hz, 100hz, 156hz, 220hz, 311hz, 440hz, 622hz, 880hz, 
       #       1250hz, 1750hz, 25000hz, 50000hz, 10000hz, 20000hz
       #range: -70 to 30
        controls [ -1 -1 -1 -1 -5 -10 -20 -17 -12 -7 -6 -5 -5 0 0 ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

[COLOR="Blue"]**Note 1**: If you are on Intrepid, this file will not exist - that's ok. Simply paste into the new file and save normally.
**Note 2**: If you have multiple sound cards, the section of this text marked in blue may need to be modified slightly. If you have problems, ask in the thread.[/COLOR]

3. Edit /etc/pulse/default.pa:```
$ gksudo gedit /etc/pulse/default.pa
```

Find the following line marked in blue:
```
### Load audio drivers statically (it's probably better to not load
### these drivers manually, but instead use module-hal-detect --
### see below -- for doing this automatically)
[COLOR="Blue"]#load-module module-alsa-sink[/COLOR]
#load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:1,0
#load-module module-oss device="/dev/dsp" sink_name=output source_name=input
```

Change the line in blue to the following, and save:```
load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalized
```
[COLOR="Blue"]**Note:** Be sure to also remove the comment (#) at the beginning of the line.[/COLOR]

4. Log out & back in for changes to take effect!

That's all folks!

[SIZE="2"]**Changelog**[/SIZE]
[SIZE="1"]*Any notable changes to the guide will be mentioned here.*
v4.0 - 13/11/08 - Complete re-write. Completely reorganized; Hardy & Intrepid guides merged. Yes, I know it's ridiculously long :P
v4.1 - 16/11/08 - Corrected some errors in the equalizer section, re-arranged guide to avoid problems with enabling ALSA plugins. Hardy users who followed the earlier revision of this guide: please run "asoundconf set-pulseaudio" to resolve any issues.
v4.2 - 23/11/08 - Added a step in Part C; due to a bug in Intrepid, users should verify their PCM volume is not muted.
v4.3 - 11/12/08 - Added a small note for OSSv4 users & information regarding PulseAudio-compatible Audacity packages available for testing.
v4.4 - 06/01/09 - Fixed typo in Skype configuration section.[/SIZE]

### Post by ProNux on 2009-02-21

### Post by rdnk on 2009-02-22
For the record, I think Pulseaudio is a great enhancement to Linux audio. Now, I recently installed Hardy Heron, and had to struggle a while to get it output sound through my chipset's (HDA Intel) S/PDIF, but eventually found it out by following this quide, and reading from some other thread, that you have to manually add the device to /etc/pulse/default.pa:
```
# intel hda digital audio
load-module module-alsa-sink device=hw:1,1
```
As you can see, I have two sound cards, so chipset lists as second card, and digital is the second channel. So device is hw:1,1.

Now the last problem I have, is that VMware sound stutters quite a bit. I managed to put virtual machines' sound through Pulseaudio OSS wrapper by following this guide: [http://communities.vmware.com/thread/163605](http://communities.vmware.com/thread/163605). At the end of the guide he suggests trying out different values for default fragments. However, dispite of trying different combinations, I've not had much success with the problem. So I'm asking, has anyone figured out appropriate values with hardware similar to mine? 

Btw, I don't think, that adjusting these values is something, that end user should have to do. Are problems with VMware already fixed in recent PA releases?

---

### Post by Milos_SD on 2009-02-25
Hi all. I installed pulseaudio 0.9.14 from this ppa:
[https://launchpad.net/~mieszkoslusarczyk/+archive/ppa](https://launchpad.net/~mieszkoslusarczyk/+archive/ppa)

And now, my equalized setup doesn't work. PulseAudio deamon doens't start. Here is a part of log that says what goes wrong:

```

pulseaudio[3094]: alsa-util.c: Failed to set hardware parameters on plug:equalized: No such file or directory
pulseaudio[3094]: module.c: Failed to load  module "module-alsa-sink" (argument: "device=equalized"): initialization failed.
pulseaudio[3094]: main.c: Module load failed.
pulseaudio[3094]: main.c: Failed to initialize daemon.

```

If I comment out this: load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalized sound is working, but w/o equalized output.
Is there a way to fix this?

---

### Post by jwmislan on 2009-02-27
> **Milos_SD said:**
> Hi all. I installed pulseaudio 0.9.14 from this ppa:
[https://launchpad.net/~mieszkoslusarczyk/+archive/ppa]("https://launchpad.net/%7Emieszkoslusarczyk/+archive/ppa")

And now, my equalized setup doesn't work. PulseAudio deamon doens't start. Here is a part of log that says what goes wrong:

```

pulseaudio[3094]: alsa-util.c: Failed to set hardware parameters on plug:equalized: No such file or directory
pulseaudio[3094]: module.c: Failed to load  module "module-alsa-sink" (argument: "device=equalized"): initialization failed.
pulseaudio[3094]: main.c: Module load failed.
pulseaudio[3094]: main.c: Failed to initialize daemon.

```If I comment out this: load-module module-alsa-sink device=equalized sound is working, but w/o equalized output.
Is there a way to fix this?

Hey Milos
I'm just guessing but -
Did you update your ladspa package when you upgraded alsa & pulse versions ??

You have both, .default.pa, and .asoundrc in your home directory? so that reinstalls don't effect your modifications.
For some reason ' alsa-util.c fails to set hardware parms to plug:equalized '
like it doesn't understand - or can't find something.

Maybe do some similiar checks like these - process of elimination - will isolate the problem. & keep track of what you do for future help post/s.

Jwm

---

### Post by Milos_SD on 2009-02-27
default.pa is in /etc/pulse/ and I use asound.conf in /etc/ folder for EQ settings. Maybe I editied asound.conf and messed it up, but I can't remember. That day when I downgraded all packages I upgraded, it didn't worked too. I was trying all day to get it to work, and late at night I found what was the problem - somehow this line in asound.conf:

```
pcm.equalizer {
  type ladspa

```
was
```

pcm.equalized {
  type ladspa

```

Maybe that was the problem with upgraded packages too, I don't know. I'll try to upgrade again these days.

I didn't upgraded ladspa package.

---

### Post by jwmislan on 2009-02-27
> **Milos_SD said:**
> default.pa is in /etc/pulse/ and I use asound.conf in /etc/ folder for EQ settings. Maybe I editied asound.conf and messed it up, but I can't remember. That day when I downgraded all packages I upgraded, it didn't worked too. I was trying all day to get it to work, and late at night I found what was the problem - somehow this line in asound.conf:

```
pcm.equalizer {
  type ladspa

```was
```

pcm.equalized {
  type ladspa

```Maybe that was the problem with upgraded packages too, I don't know. I'll try to upgrade again these days.

I didn't upgraded ladspa package.

default.pa from /etc/pulse folder - would be best if you have your own in your home folder like the instructions page 1 - equalizer section suggests.

also I would always keep my own .asoundrc for any personal preferences/config-modifications in my home folder that over-rides the /etc/alsa.conf file.
Notice the names are different /etc/asound.conf and your /home/user version - .asoundrc   - with the dot that means its a hidden file.  

You need to - make sure to set your nautilus file-browser view, to show _hidden_ files so you can see them.

Now go back to page 1 of this tutoral - the quualizer section, to be sure you have the code spelled out corectly in you own /home/Milos~$ .asoundrc  file.

you can copy the /etc/pulse/default.pa to your home directioy too.

like this,  open a terminal,  I like xterm so -
xterm 
cp /etc/pulse/default.pa /home/YourName~$ .default.pa

Also the /etc/alsa.conf that you edited
xterm 
cp /etc/alsa.conf /home/YourName~$ .asoundrc

You may want to comment out the changes you made to the /etc/alsa.conf 
so it doesn't conflict with your personal home version.
You could also delete it.
sudo rm /etc/alsa.conf
You should really go over the instructions in this tutoral the entire page 1 as a good guidline.   

PS.
because configuration files in your home directory are customarily, in linux, supposed to have the dot before them to make them hidden - private/personal - config files

hope this is of some help for you   

Jwm

---

### Post by PlatoDan on 2009-02-28
Hey, I've been looking around the forums trying to find a solution to this problem for a while now, but I can't seem to find anything. 

Basically everything starts out shiny on my build. No problems whatsoever... My issue pops up after about a day of uptime, when audio begins to not work when I open a new program. (But if I have firefox or something up the whole time it keeps working.) 

This has been happening for a while and I found somewhere a fix is to end the pulse process... It does cause sound to come back in programs that were not working, but it leaves some problems of its own. 

If I do this, Firefox's audio (Most seemingly related to flash) stops working properly after a while. All flash videos will not play well, they will be garbled and the sound will skip consistently. (So bad you can't even understand it.) Often it will just stop trying three seconds into the playback. The way to fix this is that I just need to restart firefox... Then it works again for a few hours and the problem repeats. 

Is there anything I can do to either fix pulse audio completely so I don't need to kill it or at least something that will make Firefox function better? 

Thanks! :)

---

Audigy Analog/Digital Output Jack checkbox was checked - apparently that was a problem.  So now I have sound.  Amarok doesn't play anything, but I imagine that's another problem entirely.

### Post by renebs on 2009-03-03
Thanks for the post, it really helped. 

But I still have a problem: I have installed ORCA, together with it I also installed "speech-dispatcher'.

Now, my problem is; if speech-dispatcher is running (and once installed it starts up every boot) I can't play any audio.

My (lame) solution for my problem has been so far (in case I don't need speech-dispatcher): sudo /etc/init.d/speech-dispatcher stop, do a logout from gnome, and once back every audio application I cared to test works fine.

I followed your manual and this hasn't solved the problem (I figured the part with "shared" audio would work. 

If you have any help it would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks

### Post by lobo-tuerto on 2009-03-04
Hi guys,

Everything was working fine in my computer and then suddenly (maybe) a couple of days ago the sound stopped working.
I was a user of the route: "sudo alsa force-reload" until it was good no more. The thing is that I can hear a "clicking" noise when I run that command so that probably means it isn't a hardware issue, but a software one? I can hear that sound when the machine is booting too.

Anyway I decided to go with this guide and my problem seems to be described here:
> # The application does not play audio and does list an entry in the Playback tab;
- the application is using PulseAudio but there is a problem, such as: a bug in PulseAudio, a problem with your ALSA kernel module or libraries, or your PCM/Master volume is muted.

So here is the data of my machine:

### Post by KeithUK on 2009-03-05
I've got an Audigy2 card attached to a 7.1 sound system running on an AMD64 box.

I've been getting very similar problems to those described here.

By following the guide I've managed to get 2 channel stereo working ok (although I think I might just be using ALSA and bypassing Pulseaudio altogether - trouble is I don't have enough expertise in this area to say conclusively).

Been hacking around trying to get full 7.1 surround sound and was on the point of giving up when I found this; [http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-595412.html](http://ubuntuforums.org/archive/index.php/t-595412.html)

Specifically:
> I installed the Hardy RC today and had similar problems with my 5.1, all I needed to do was uncomment the line 'default-sample-channels' in the file /etc/pulse/daemon.conf and set the value from 2 to 6 for this line. Then I just set everything to use PulseAudio in System>Preferences>Sound.

The pulseaudio mixer now has 8 channels instead of 2 and I'm getting full surround sound.

Only problem now is pulseaudio keeps crashing with rhythmbox but looking at the bug lists that might be a genuine pulseaudio/rhythmbox bug.

However I've mucked about with my sound configs so much that the pulseaudio/rhythmbox problem may well be something I've done.

I'm going to spend some time trying to tidy up my system and remove some of the cruft I've undoubtedly generated and see if that helps.

If and when I get everything to work I'll post back here if anyone's interested.

Oh, by the way one of my problems was the onboard sound card was being picked up by the sound system instead of my Audigy2 card. If you don't want the onboard sound card at all just switching it off in the BIOS will often not work (Linux is FAR too clever! :) ). I had to add the following to /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist;
> # supress onboard sound card
blacklist snd-via82xx
blacklist snd_via82xx

then remember to run
> sudo update-initramfs
otherwise you'll spend hours wondering why the module is still loading (just like I did :oops:) however when I finally figured it out I suddenly had sound - albeit just 2 channels.

### Post by blausand on 2009-03-18
Thank you a lot, mister! 
Helped me out of that firefox3 silence with my Terratec Aureon 5.1 .

@Everybody else please note: After following the howto, audio seemed to have been broken all together. Both my collegue and i had to switch the audio settings hin und her several times before it suddenly worked out fine for the whole pulseAudio system. The autodetect feature didn't work at all, and in the end it came down to work with "ALSA PCM on front:1 (USB Audio) via DMA". Also, the default flag had to be checked twice for the same Device!

blausand

[http://pastebin.com/m6c79f149](http://pastebin.com/m6c79f149)

And my /etc/pulse/default.pa looks like this:
[http://pastebin.com/mc3bff2](http://pastebin.com/mc3bff2)

If someone know how can I fix this, please tell. :(

I've updated the guide to add a note for Jaunty users, please read it.

Regarding your question, there is an alternative way to get LADSPA output in newer versions of PulseAudio (that wasn't suitable for Hardy or Intrepid, due to instability).

See: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5247472&postcount=274](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=5247472&postcount=274)*

However, consider yourself alone on this - I won't be giving support to any questions from Jaunty users (especially since PulseAudio is still being tweaked heavily).

*Note: you can probably skip step 2 (i.e., do not disable module-suspend-on-idle).

### Post by oldsoundguy on 2009-03-24
This works BUT ... still the same damn problem.  NO master volume control on the DIGITAL OUT of a Creative 5.1.
Have to rely on the volume control of the input item or program .. and that is impossible with the system sounds!

(Hardy LTS)

ANY CLUES FOLKS?

ADD:  Will installing the software EQ give me a master volume control?  Have not installed such as I feed a digital surround system that HAS a multiband EQ as part of the setup!

---

### Post by markbuntu on 2009-03-24
> **oldsoundguy said:**
> This works BUT ... still the same damn problem.  NO master volume control on the DIGITAL OUT of a Creative 5.1.
Have to rely on the volume control of the input item or program .. and that is impossible with the system sounds!

(Hardy LTS)

ANY CLUES FOLKS?

ADD:  Will installing the software EQ give me a master volume control?  Have not installed such as I feed a digital surround system that HAS a multiband EQ as part of the setup!

This is from the pulseaudio mailing list from yesterday.
> 
Hi,

My audio device has an IEC958 output as well as analog out.  I set up
two module-alsa-sink's so that i can direct streams to either output,
but I found that I couldn't control the volume of each sink
independently.  I was able to use asound.conf to create a softvol
control for the digital out.  However, module-alsa-sink doesn't have
any way to select a particular mixer control.  Since both pcms are
part of the same card they both end up using the Master control.

That said, you could maybe set up a softvol in your asoundrc for the hardware address, ie hw0.3 or whatever your digital is and maybe control it separately from an alsa mixer but maybe not. I don't know about system sounds, I have them turned off myself.

---

### Post by oldsoundguy on 2009-03-24
Actually, I have most of the system sounds OFF including the obnoxious desktop load noise .. the bongos are cool for sign in plus they do not show up in the system sounds anyway and are NOT that loud.  The ONE system sound I have to have is my eMail alert.  Rest went bye bye.  
BUT, that alert will tear my head off if I have the surround amp cranked up and listening to some tunes.  (have to have the alert for eMails as I do on line consultation.) I did manage to get the master volume to work on items I am running through the Alsa Mixer now .. that helps a bunch. (as to HOW I did it?  NOT A CLUE!!  It just "happened"!)

I did install the equalizer .. and, even though this is a desktop and not a laptop, it DID improve the sound .. smoothed it out and took off some of the edge. (If I am getting too esoteric, sorry, retired audio engineer .. LOL)

---

### Post by markbuntu on 2009-03-24
I did some sound engineering in my day also. my fav was working with punkrockers. Live recording was a total adventure, everything turned up to 11, 8 mics into a 1/2 track. I needed to use a dbx range expander just to get some separation when I mixed it down.

Anyway, the answer to bad sound is good speakers. As you should know, if you need to eq for anything but the room your speakers are crap. I got some little Klipsch KG1.2s and a tiny Onkyo reciever for my computer. I leave them flat.

Anyway, FYI the new pulseaudio has  a separate playback control for system sounds but it is not working right, at least not right now in Jaunty. Koala will have pulse0.9.15 or 16. Pulse 0.9.15 is very close to what pulse should be and a great leap from 0.9.10 so you should just be patient. 

All things come to he who waits.

---

### Post by oldsoundguy on 2009-03-24
Bass, mid and high are not sufficient to eq to a room!! ESPECIALLY if there is a wall of GLASS!! LOL 
Good speakers or not .. and most are NOT flat nor is the room.  Without and EQ, there are holes and hotspots in the playback .. especially in surround (a lot of my tunes are recorded in surround!)
That is one thing I miss from windows as I had a program with a 23 band EQ in it!
(but an update about six months ago on XP killed the digital out on a 7.1 card and both Windows and Creative are passing the buck as to who should fix the issue .. thus far neither is moving (typical)!!

It will be interesting to see what transpires in the sound area .. since Ubuntu dropped the ball on video in 8.10 and just "left things out" (like being able to DO anything with the xorg file other than look at a bunch of generic terms!)(dropped back to 8.04 for that specific reason!)

I am really glad the OP put this thread up, as it DID solve the majority of my sound issues!

---

### Post by wheresawhatnow@gmail.com on 2009-03-29
Hi all,

My sound stopped working on me about a week ago after a round of updates.  I had previously gotten it working thanks to this post, so I figured I'd try it's suggestions again, but this time with no luck.  

I've also tried this thread's suggestions: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=997506](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=997506), but again to no avail.  

I'm running UbuntuStudio 8.04 i386, which I think is pretty much the same thing as Hardy Heron i386 with a few other bells and whistles.  I'm using an M-audio Fast Track Pro USB device as my sound card.  

I had the same setup working on another computer until my motherboard died on me, so I had to switch desktops.  And as I said, this new desktop was working fine until about a week ago.  Also, when I select USB audio #1 as my playback device in System->Preferences->Sound, and hit test, I can here the test tone through the Fast Track.  So, in short, I don't think it's a hardware issue with the soundcard itself.  

I don't know enough about ubuntu/pulseaudio to have any idea of what to try next, so if anyone has any suggestions for me I'd really appreciate it.  Here's the listing of my sound devices and the verbose output from pulseaudio.

### Post by bpont on 2009-03-31
I came to your guide by way of Google after experiencing Firefox crashes while trying to view flash videos on YouTube and elsewhere.  This was caused by libflashsupport, which I consequently uninstalled.

Long story short, I used your guide and successfully solved the firefox crashing problem and the no audio in flash when vlc or rhythmbox is playing problem.

Everything seemed perfect until I fired up Rosetta Stone (language learning software for Windows XP that I run using WINE).  The application opened properly and proceeded to the lesson, but once it came to actually playing a sound, the application just hung.  I should mention that I never had a problem using this software under WINE until I made the changes in your guide.

I went to the troubleshooting section and made sure the alsa driver was chosen in audo config tab...and even tested it by playing a sound.  It didn't solve my problem with Rosetta Stone though.  When I open pulseaudio volume control and check the stream data when Rosetta Stone is running, I get this...but there's no sound playing and there's only one device (default) I can stream to:

[IMG]http://i44.tinypic.com/10f164p.png[/IMG]

So now I'm at a loss for what to do next in order to correct this.  It's really important for me to have this resolved because this program is really expensive and I use it everyday.

Here is the information I can give you:

Hardy Heron i386

$ aplay -l
**** List of PLAYBACK Hardware Devices ****
card 0: CMI8738 [C-Media CMI8738], device 0: CMI8738 [C-Media PCI DAC/ADC]
  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: CMI8738 [C-Media CMI8738], device 1: CMI8738 [C-Media PCI 2nd DAC]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: CMI8738 [C-Media CMI8738], device 2: CMI8738 [C-Media PCI IEC958]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-01
> **bpont said:**
> Everything seemed perfect until I fired up Rosetta Stone (language learning software for Windows XP that I run using WINE).  The application opened properly and proceeded to the lesson, but once it came to actually playing a sound, the application just hung.  I should mention that I never had a problem using this software under WINE until I made the changes in your guide.

I recommend:
a) Upgrade WINE to the latest version. See: [http://winehq.org/download/deb](http://winehq.org/download/deb)
b) If the newer version of WINE does not work, change the audio driver in winecfg to OSS, then attempt to launch WINE through the padsp wrapper. See Appendix C of this guide.

Unfortunately PulseAudio and ALSA are somewhat outdated in Hardy, and some issues are present that can only be resolved by upgrading. You could try Intrepid (if you have a fast connection and lots of RAM, I recommend you boot from the livecd and install WINE into the live environment to test), or perhaps Jaunty, if you're willing to become a beta tester.

---

### Post by GepettoBR on 2009-04-01
Psyke, do you know the status of PulseAudio in Jaunty? I haven't gotten around to testing the Beta just yet.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-01
> **GepettoBR said:**
> Psyke, do you know the status of PulseAudio in Jaunty? I haven't gotten around to testing the Beta just yet.

I'm using the beta release of Jaunty and I've experienced zero issues with PulseAudio. There are still some problems with the Ubuntu kernel (high latency) which means that the "glitch-free" code is disabled - otherwise it's working fine. Manual configuration (such as the steps in this guide) are no longer necessary.

I recommend you browse the [appropriate]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=352") forum, and see the [stickied]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1084919") thread dealing with PulseAudio (since I'm not involved in development, there's not much useful information I can give ;)).

---

ty for the info and link. H?ave a nice Pink Forum day.

### Post by Josephxfs on 2009-04-01
Okay I'm very sorry if this has been asked before but looking through all 100 and some odd pages seems like a daunting task at the moment.  

I've set my settings to that of the op (except the equalizer guide)  
Using Ubuntu 8.04.2 I386 etc Flash player 10
I've thankfully got the flash and sound programs working together so I can listen to both but now unfortunately I can't use sound recorder yet I can hear my mic through my speakers and can see the source in the pulse audio applet.  But when I use Sound recorder it only gives master as an input and records the outputs of the master volume(programs etc.).  Is there a post about this problem in this thread?

Also upon trying to use ubuntu's volume monitor app(located in Applications >Sound&Video) Pulse audio itself crashes with a segfault(when playing from flashvids usually)

It seems making OSS my sound capture under system>preferences>sound fixes the mic input problem Supposedly Although I haven't tested anything other than sound recorder.  Could this cause problems? could anyone help??

But when I use Sound recorder it only gives master as an input and records the outputs of the master volume(programs etc.).  Is there a post about this problem in this thread?

Also upon trying to use ubuntu's volume monitor app(located in Applications >Sound&Video) Pulse audio itself crashes with a segfault(when playing from flashvids usually)

It seems making OSS my sound capture under system>preferences>sound fixes the mic input problem Supposedly Although I haven't tested anything other than sound recorder.  Could this cause problems? could anyone help??

It's not recommended to record sound using PulseAudio as crashes can occur frequently with certain applications - use the default ALSA recording device instead. I'll update the guide to reflect this information.

Edit: please review Part A, Step 4.

### Post by bpont on 2009-04-02
> **psyke83 said:**
> I recommend:
a) Upgrade WINE to the latest version. See: [http://winehq.org/download/deb](http://winehq.org/download/deb)
b) If the newer version of WINE does not work, change the audio driver in winecfg to OSS, then attempt to launch WINE through the padsp wrapper. See Appendix C of this guide.


Thanks for the great advice.  I solved the problem by simply changing the audio driver in winecfg from alsa to oss.

### Post by Auslegung on 2009-04-02
Great How-to, very detailed, but I've got a huge problem.  I am using 8.10 with Adam's eee kernel on my eee PC 1000h.  My system was working, I even had Skype working correctly, but my Rhythmbox started skipping today.  I found your how-to via google and followed the steps.  At 'A' step 5, when typing pavucontrol, I get the error.  I then typed pulseaudio & pavucontrol and the terminal said 

```
The program 'pulseaudio' is currently not installed.  You can install it by typing:
sudo apt-get install pulseaudio
```

Very weird, not sure why pulseaudio wasn't installed, so I did it.  Then I typed pavucontrol and got the error, so I typed pulseaudio & pavucontrol again, and got this

```
[1] 10379
W: ltdl-bind-now.c: Failed to find original dlopen loader.
W: main.c: setrlimit(RLIMIT_NICE, (31, 31)) failed: Operation not permitted
W: main.c: setrlimit(RLIMIT_RTPRIO, (9, 9)) failed: Operation not permitted
E: alsa-util.c: Error opening PCM device hw:0: Device or resource busy
E: module.c: Failed to load  module "module-alsa-sink" (argument: "device_id=0 sink_name=alsa_output.pci_8086_27d8_sound_card_0_alsa_playback_0"): initialization failed.
ALSA lib control.c:909:(snd_ctl_open_noupdate) Invalid CTL front:0
```

I followed steps 1-5 again and when typing pulseaudio & pavucontrol got this

```
[2] 11039
W: ltdl-bind-now.c: Failed to find original dlopen loader.
E: pid.c: Daemon already running.
E: main.c: pa_pid_file_create() failed.
[2]+  Exit 1                  pulseaudio
```

I can see you're very busy with this thread alone, but any help would be much appreciated.  I'm very pissed b/c I had finally, after more than a year of trying to get Skype to work properly, gotten it to work just a few days ago.  I should learn that if it ain't broke, don't fix it.

After switching to Pulse, I get a 5 second delay on the login drums.  If I don't wait for the drums, I get a black screen hang-up while the desktop loads.  I didn't experience this problem until I worked with Pulse.  I'd appreciate any help with the errors encountered on startup.

### Post by tobreme on 2009-04-10
Re the steps to follow for Hardy Heron :
2. Update your repositories and upgrade packages:
Code:

$ sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get dist-upgrade

On today April 8 2009: the Macromedia Flash download gives an error saying checksum does not validate and the Flash install halts. Manually installing as root - su install_flash... does work however. Do this before running the filal step of 

Code:

$ asoundconf set-pulseaudio

This doc is a gem and thanks to the authors and contributors.

### Post by jamesisin on 2009-04-11
phreaknite - Check things like:

Are the speakers plugged in or attached correctly?

Are they connected to a power supply?

Can you get audio by attaching to some other audio device (headphones or mini-plug to RCA connecting to stereo receiver)?

Do you get the login sound?

### Post by Clancy_s on 2009-04-12
I'm using Intrepid Ibex amd54, installed 3 days ago on a new desktop.  With the default setting sound worked, but at max volume all the time, no response to the slider even when it was moved all the way down to mute.  The noise levels were causing distortion in our speakers even with the volume knob on the speaker set really low and we use sound quite a bit so I decided to try this fix rather than waiting for the release version of jaunty.

After running the fixes I'm in position C - the playback is listed in the playback but there is no sound, with Rhythmbox, Totem or Firefox (flash vids play without sound) and I get no system sounds.

As suggested I suspect the problem is in the  PCM mixer - although the volume icon on the task bar doesn't show as muted the output tab does show it as muted in pulse audio volume control.  However I've not been able to get alsamixer to fix it.

Initially alsamixer showed sound as muted for the master  <IEC958>   but on for all the subsidiary controls ( IEC958C, IEC958F, IEC958R, IEC958U, Analog C, Analog F, Analog R, Analog S) although some of the subsidiaries were set at 0.  I've managed to set the master <IEC958> to 00 instead of mute using the m key but I can't raise the volume on the slider for it (arrow keys, which work for all the other entries), all the other entries are set at 70%.

Is there any other way I can unmute the master control?  Or is that merely a symptom of a deeper problem.

*As an alternative how do I go about reverting the changes?* ; max volume was better than no sound at all and I'll wait and see how Jaunty works.  I've tried setting system > preferences > sounds to alsa and OSS manually, still I have no sound.  Replacing the new files with the old versions of .pulse and /etc/pulse also hasn't restored sound, I didn't have the asound files

eta1: the speakers and connections are all still as they were when I had sound.  I just booted back into winXP, sound works there with the same connections, I've lost sound for all users, not just the one I ran the changes from.  I even tried making a new user - still no sound.

eta2: the old and new versions of /etc/pulse are the same, ~/.pulse is different but I dunno if that means anything since neither version works now and users I haven't altered still don't have sound.  I presume the switch from 100% to 0% sound is due to a change in drivers but I don't know which nor how to revert without a reinstall which I'd prefer to avoid - I've already reinstalled winXP and done one install of ubuntu and I'm running out of Easter.

eta3: I found the solution in this thread

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=997506](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=997506)

I didn't need to install anything extra though, and I only had to do part of the stuff in the setup section.  My problem was the IEC958, which I gather is digital output (my speakers are analogue).

In summary:

right click on the volume icon, select volume control.  This opened up alsamixer with a graphical control.  The only slider available was IEC958.  I used preferences to add a slider for ALSA front (which is how my speakers are connected, change that according to your needs) and remove the IEC958 slider and added a switches panel and used that to deselect IEC958.  This gave me sound, that I could control via the slider in volume control or the individual apps, but not via the slider on the panel, which is where I started.

To get the volume slider on the panel to work I right clicked on it again, this time selected prefences.  It was set to control IEC958.  I changed that to ALSA front, closed preferences and the volume slider also works as it should.

My problem solved.

### Post by DesiArnez6 on 2009-04-19
*I just found a solution, added to the bottom:

Hardy Heron i386

card 0: Intel [HDA Intel], device 0: AD198x Analog [AD198x Analog]
  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: Intel [HDA Intel], device 1: AD198x Digital [AD198x Digital]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0

Problem: After applying the fixes on this thread for Hardy Heron, I was finally able to hear streams in Firefox. My problem was that I had no audio when playing youtube videos (Flash 9), which before applying this fix,  that was ALL I was able to hear. Youtube only, but no streams in Firefox.

I attempted the steps in Appendix A, and the response I got was between "C" and "D". I had no audio (in youtube), but an ALSA entry was very very rapidly appearing and disappearing in the Playback tab.

***Solution:

So, to get sound in both, Youtube (Flash 9) AND Firefox streams, I found the following, and it worked. Post #7 From: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=732520](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=732520)

*Note: after applying all the steps below, my computer did crash and I couldn't even restart with an "RSEIUB", but, after a hard reboot, everything was fine.


Direct quote from post:
"I performed a fresh install of Ubuntu 8.04 and everything was going great until last night. I was notified that there were 40 updates to pull down, and being the diligent techie that I am I went ahead with the install. Shortly thereafter I noticed that my flash videos had no audio. Standard video and audio files on my PC had audio, just not flash. Thanks to panholt for this post, the issue is resolved. Just follow these steps:

1. Open a terminal window via Applications -> Accessories -> Terminal.
2. Type in the below command and hit the enter key:

sudo apt-get install libflashsupport

3. Enter your password when prompted and let the package install.
4. Close the terminal window.
5. Open Sound Preferences via System -> Preferences -> Sound.
6. Change the first four drop downs to PulseAudio Sound Server.
7. Click the Close button.
8. Restart any applications that utilize sound (FireFox, Totem, RhythmBox, etc).

All applications should now be using one, unified sound system, and there should no longer be any conflicts that cause certain applications to drop audio all together."

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-20
> **DesiArnez6 said:**
> *I just found a solution, added to the bottom:

Hardy Heron i386

card 0: Intel [HDA Intel], device 0: AD198x Analog [AD198x Analog]
  Subdevices: 0/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0
card 0: Intel [HDA Intel], device 1: AD198x Digital [AD198x Digital]
  Subdevices: 1/1
  Subdevice #0: subdevice #0

Problem: After applying the fixes on this thread for Hardy Heron, I was finally able to hear streams in Firefox. My problem was that I had no audio when playing youtube videos (Flash 9), which before applying this fix,  that was ALL I was able to hear. Youtube only, but no streams in Firefox.

I attempted the steps in Appendix A, and the response I got was between "C" and "D". I had no audio (in youtube), but an ALSA entry was very very rapidly appearing and disappearing in the Playback tab.

***Solution:

So, to get sound in both, Youtube (Flash 9) AND Firefox streams, I found the following, and it worked. Post #7 From: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=732520](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=732520)

*Note: after applying all the steps below, my computer did crash and I couldn't even restart with an "RSEIUB", but, after a hard reboot, everything was fine.


Direct quote from post:
"I performed a fresh install of Ubuntu 8.04 and everything was going great until last night. I was notified that there were 40 updates to pull down, and being the diligent techie that I am I went ahead with the install. Shortly thereafter I noticed that my flash videos had no audio. Standard video and audio files on my PC had audio, just not flash. Thanks to panholt for this post, the issue is resolved. Just follow these steps:

1. Open a terminal window via Applications -> Accessories -> Terminal.
2. Type in the below command and hit the enter key:

sudo apt-get install libflashsupport

3. Enter your password when prompted and let the package install.
4. Close the terminal window.
5. Open Sound Preferences via System -> Preferences -> Sound.
6. Change the first four drop downs to PulseAudio Sound Server.
7. Click the Close button.
8. Restart any applications that utilize sound (FireFox, Totem, RhythmBox, etc).

All applications should now be using one, unified sound system, and there should no longer be any conflicts that cause certain applications to drop audio all together."

The libflashsupport library is horribly unstable - it'll cause Firefox to randomly hang when navigating pages with Flash content. Nobody should be using this library, and this guide deliberately instructs users to remove it for this reason. If you don't believe me, see the comments on the [extensive bug report]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/192888").

PulseAudio and Flash 9 simply cannot co-operate well together. Upgrade to Flash 10 and remove libflashsupport, and your issues will disappear.

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-23
Hey folks,

The guide now supports the Jaunty Jackalope (9.04) release. PulseAudio now works correctly out of the box, but it may be necessary for some users to delete PulseAudio user configuration if they are dist-upgrading - hence it is supported in this guide.

---

### Post by Warai on 2009-04-23
I just installed 9.04 Netbook Remix on my Asus Eee 1000h. The sound all works, as well as my microphone, the only problem is that it is unreasonably quiet. I have maxed out all of the volume settings I can find but it still remains too soft to hear almost anything even with in ear headphones. I am using PulseAudio across the board for both output and my mic input. Any way to get a boost on my sound?

I apologize if this has been addressed earlier, just don't have time to read the 121 pages of this thread.

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-23
> **Warai said:**
> I just installed 9.04 Netbook Remix on my Asus Eee 1000h. The sound all works, as well as my microphone, the only problem is that it is unreasonably quiet. I have maxed out all of the volume settings I can find but it still remains too soft to hear almost anything even with in ear headphones. I am using PulseAudio across the board for both output and my mic input. Any way to get a boost on my sound?

I apologize if this has been addressed earlier, just don't have time to read the 121 pages of this thread.

Try:
```
$ alsamixer -Dhw
```

Set the appropriate sliders to max (PCM, Master, perhaps also Surround or others too).

---

### Post by Warai on 2009-04-23
Worked like a charm, thank you so much.

---

### Post by Warai on 2009-04-23
One more questions, while the previous post worked great for output, my mic input is still quite low and there is no slider in the AlsaMixer for it. Any way to get it to pick up a bit more?

---

### Post by Warai on 2009-04-23
I see that there is a capture section but am unaware of how to access it. I apologize for my relative naivety.

---

### Post by Warai on 2009-04-23
Actually capture slider is maxed out with still very low input sound.

### Post by barisurum on 2009-04-23

```
pulseaudio -k
```

and restart it with

```
pulseaudio
```

then for another period I have sound, then goes silent. When it goes silent after I restart pulse and when I try to play something I get this error in terminal:

> E: module-alsa-sink.c: Failed to set hardware parameters: Operation not permitted

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-24
> **stimpack said:**
> Same, Part A failed on Kubuntu Jaunty, on installing libao-pulse. It recommends installing libao2 instead but that will remove alsa lib, which works for World of Warcraft, the only app I have that sound works in, and I'm loathe to lose that.

As GepettoBR mentioned, Kubuntu isn't configured to use PulseAudio. I don't recommend you install it on your system - KDE4 uses its own audio framework (Phonon), and I don't know how it interacts with PulseAudio.

### Post by Ticketoride on 2009-04-25
> **psyke83 said:**
> 5. Open the PulseAudio Volume Control application ("pavucontrol", or you can launch "Applications/Sound & Video/PulseAudio Device Chooser" and select Volume Control from this applet's menu). In the Output Devices section you will see a listing of the playback devices available on your system. Right-click on the entry that you desire to be made the default playback device on your system and enable the "Default" checkmark. Similarly, navigate to Input Devices, then right-click on the device you wish to set as your default input device (microphone), and ensure the "Default" setting is checked. Close the application when you're finished.
The only Input/Output Devices found were "Null Devices". 

Checking the Installation History, on a Hunch I found, and removed the **xmms2** Audio Player and all associated Libraries.

Thereafter, the actual Onboard Audio Device showed up under Input/Output Devices of "pavucontrol", and Audio played once again.

Excellent Guide, very doubtful the Audio would have been restored any other Way.

---

### Post by rabbit251 on 2009-04-25
I think my system may have been OK already, but following Part A doesn't seem to have hurt my 9.04, at the very least. (May have helped with soundcard sharing across applications and levels stability, too.) The instructions were clear and well-written, thanks!

> Note: If you are greeted with the error "Connection failed: Connection refused", manually launch PulseAudio before opening the PulseAudio Volume Control application:
Code:

```
$ pulseaudio & pavucontrol
```

Just a note that I had to restart the session before I could get past these errors, even using the above command.

[COLOR="Lime"]Update (one hour later): OMG! Actually, Part A saved my life! I poured twenty hours this week into desperately searching for a solution or workaround to an issue with my mic volume (hopelessly low, esp. on Skype) with little luck. It was all magically fixed after following these instructions and rebooting my machine! Thank you![/COLOR]

---

### Post by tcrisan on 2009-04-26
Pulseaudio was working on Hardy and Intrepid but since upgrading to Jaunty, I have no sound locally and can only play sound files using a KDE application (amarok) with the sound redirected to another sound server.

If someone could help me troubleshoot the problem, it would be much appreciated.  I have only default pulseaudio configuration files from Jaunty.  When I issue the commands:

killall pulseaudio
pulseaudio

I receive the following output:

I: caps.c: Limited capabilities successfully to CAP_SYS_NICE.

### Post by car-zonecar-zone on 2009-04-26
Some time ago, Mandriva decided to install the PulseAudio sound server by default in its distribution. Personally I did not really like the decision to activate PulseAudio by default in all desktop environments: I have been using Alsa with great satisfaction now for years, and I did not have any problem with that, so having to depend on yet another service did seem overkill to me. Nevertheless, I let PulseAudio be activated on my system too, in order to help as much as possible with bug hunting.

---

### Post by viriatus on 2009-04-26
Pulseaudio sucks

### Post by barisurum on 2009-04-26
> Pulseaudio sucks

I don't want to be rude but it does :D I have a suggestion to the developers. It appears to me they don't take into account the N. 1 rule of software development: First cleanup bugs, then put new features...

I hope pulse gets better in the future but for now I will be using jack with my usb-audio interface (delta sigma converters mmmm) for music and video (mplayer). And I will use my onbord mobo pcm converter (iyyyk) with pulse for game sounds. Farewell.

### Post by miromiro on 2009-04-27
I have just completed a fresh install of Jaunty and have some weirdness with my audio: when I get to the login prompt, I hear the little drum roll, but once I am logged in I get nothing - from any app, Totem, Rhythmbox, etc...

Output of my log file is:

```

Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Device hw:1 doesn't support 2 channels, changed to 10.
Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Device hw:1 doesn't support sample format s16le, changed to s32le.
Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Cannot find fallback mixer control "PCM" or mixer control is no combination of switch/volume.
Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Device hw:1 doesn't support 2 channels, changed to 12.
Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Device hw:1 doesn't support sample format s16le, changed to s32le.
Apr 27 18:44:00  pulseaudio[3976]: alsa-util.c: Cannot find fallback mixer control "Mic" or mixer control is no combination of switch/volume.
Apr 27 19:03:09  syslogd 1.5.0#5ubuntu3: restart.

```

I have added myself to the audio group and done the same for pulse authorizations, but I am not really getting anywhere with this. Can anyone steer me in the right direction?

EDIT: OK, some googling put me on to [this tip]("http://www.ubuntugeek.com/simple-guide-to-sound-solutions-for-hardyintrepid-and-jaunty-jackalope-users.html") - right click on the stream in PulseAudio Device Chooser and move stream to a simultaneous output. I repeated that for all the apps that require sound & I seem to be in business. It held after a reboot, so here's hoping I am good....

---

Your log indicates that some libraries that PulseAudio needs aren't installed properly. See: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/326532](https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/326532)

### Post by vwilsonuk on 2009-04-28
Hi everyone,

Until upgrading to 9.0.4 my sound was working fine (albeit with the occasional glitch where upgrades muted my PCM volume without telling anybody)!

Today I upgraded, and now my system produces no sound at all. I've followed the instructions at the start of this thread, and now have my playback devices set to "Auto detect", capture set to "ALSA" and device set to "Playback: HDA Intel STAC92xxx". (I've also tried it with device set to HDA Intel ALSA mixer and it makes no difference). 

My PulseAudio Volume Control shows applications playing sounds, and the monitors go up and down to indicate that sound is being processed and "output" (although no sound is being produced by the laptop). My PCM volume is not set to zero, or muted.

aplay -l output is as follows:

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-28
> **vwilsonuk said:**
> 
Please recommend next steps to identifying the cause of this problem?

Many thanks,


Victoria

There are two likely possibilities to explain why your sound isn't working.

a) PulseAudio is using the digital output but your speakers are connected to the analog output, or vice-versa. Ensure you have selected the correct Output Device in the PulseAudio Volume Control application.

You can also try to play sound in an application (e.g., Totem), then open the PulseAudio Volume Control application. Click on the Playback tab, and right-click on the Totem entry, choose "Move Stream..." and try changing it to another device listed.

b) One of the "hidden" mixer properties has disabled sound. Play some audio in Totem, and while it is playing, do the following:

Open alsamixer:
```
$ alsamixer -Dhw
```

Check all of the playback mixers, especially the Master, PCM, Surround, Headphone. Also you should check the (usually hidden) switches such as IEC958 and External Amplifier. I get no sound on my laptop when the External Amplifier is muted, for example.

---

### Post by vwilsonuk on 2009-04-28
Hi, 

Thanks for getting back to me on this...

> **psyke83 said:**
> There are two likely possibilities to explain why your sound isn't working.

a) PulseAudio is using the digital output but your speakers are connected to the analog output, or vice-versa. Ensure you have selected the correct Output Device in the PulseAudio Volume Control application.

You can also try to play sound in an application (e.g., Totem), then open the PulseAudio Volume Control application. Click on the Playback tab, and right-click on the Totem entry, choose "Move Stream..." and try changing it to another device listed.


There only appears to be analogue output on this system. I tried moving the stream, but there is only one device listed. There is also only one playback device listed in the sound preferences.

> **psyke83 said:**
> 
b) One of the "hidden" mixer properties has disabled sound. Play some audio in Totem, and while it is playing, do the following:

Open alsamixer:
```
$ alsamixer -Dhw
```

Check all of the playback mixers, especially the Master, PCM, Surround, Headphone. Also you should check the (usually hidden) switches such as IEC958 and External Amplifier. I get no sound on my laptop when the External Amplifier is muted, for example.

I've checked all of them and none of them are muted. I also tried switching the IEC958 between Digital and Analogue streams and it made no difference.

What next?


V

---

### Post by psyke83 on 2009-04-28
> **vwilsonuk said:**
> Hi, 

Thanks for getting back to me on this...



There only appears to be analogue output on this system. I tried moving the stream, but there is only one device listed. There is also only one playback device listed in the sound preferences.



I've checked all of them and none of them are muted. I also tried switching the IEC958 between Digital and Analogue streams and it made no difference.

What next?


V

First, try plugging in some headphones and see if you can hear audio.

Second, go to System/Preferences/Sound and change the option for "Music & Movies -> Sound Playback" to OSS. This cause GStreamer applications such as Totem to bypass PulseAudio entirely.

Close all other sound applications, and open some media in one instance of Totem. If sound does not play correctly, then your problem is not with PulseAudio (and more likely is an ALSA bug).

Note: change the Sound Playback option back to Autodetect when you're finished.

---

### Post by monstrosity on 2009-04-28
I'm completely dumbfounded.

After trying to install the PA equalizer (stupid idea, I know, since I am on 64) I was left with a seemingly unchanged PA. Everything that had worked before still worked, but there was no equalizer. I figured "oh well" and let it be. A few days later, I plugged in a USB device (which always gives at least a little trouble and I have to restart PA) and it broke PA which then said it couldn't connect. 

After reversing the steps to install the equalizer, I am left with a Pulseaudio that thinks it is working, but the only output device it sees is "Null Output" which is entirely useless. Everything displays as if it is working correctly, but I have no sound through the headphones or onboard speakers which were working perfectly before. I haven't yet tried again with the USB audio. 

I can't figure out anything to do. Help!

Edit... Fixed! All it took was deleting the old configuration files and reinstalling.

I'm on Jaunty 64 with 1.0.19 alsa and 9.15 PA.

---

### Post by vwilsonuk on 2009-04-28

> First, try plugging in some headphones and see if you can hear audio.

Second, go to System/Preferences/Sound and change the option for "Music & Movies -> Sound Playback" to OSS. This cause GStreamer applications such as Totem to bypass PulseAudio entirely.

Close all other sound applications, and open some media in one instance of Totem. If sound does not play correctly, then your problem is not with PulseAudio (and more likely is an ALSA bug).

Note: change the Sound Playback option back to Autodetect when you're finished.

Ok - the headphones don't make any difference, and neither does switching to OSS - so perhaps an ALSA bug as you've suggested. How do I go about fixing that?

Thank you again for your help on this - loads of people seem to be having sound issues with Jaunty.

V

I'm having a problem with C The application does not play audio and does list an entry in the Playback tab;
- the application is using PulseAudio but there is a problem, such as: a bug in PulseAudio, a problem with your ALSA kernel module or libraries, or your PCM/Master volume is muted.

It shows that the thing is playing but i have no sound.
Any help is apricated

### Post by tja on 2009-04-29
hoi.
upgraded to jaunty on my mythbuntu box this night and spdif is gone (again). im basically messing in the dark with the system every distri upgrade to get it working again.
maybe you can bring some light into this.

problem:
[LIST]
[*]no sound via spdif in all ubuntu apps except myth-frontend (ofc as it doesnt use pa)
[*]pavucontrol sees the analog output (=sink ?!?) only
[/LIST]

aplay -l says:
```

```
